title: +Beverage Bonanza 1.0.1
description: Beverage Bonanza (ver. 1.0.1) c1996 by Eric & Kris Holsen (VeronaEMH@aol.com) Staff Pick: This is a very well done stack, looks like a lot more fun than a social studies textbook : ) Thank you for your interest in this Verona Software product. NOTE: This is a fix of the version of this program uploaded previously. The bug that kept you from buying anything but lemons has been fixed. Beverage Bonanza is an economic simulation game designed to help students understand some basic concepts about economics through a simulated 'real-world', hands-on learning experience. The student gets to be the owner of a lemonade and hot chocolate stand. In order to run this business they will need to make a number of business decisions such as: how many materials to purchase, how much product to make, and what price to sell the product at. The choices the student makes will affect how well she/he does in the game. This program was written by a 7th and 8th grade social studies teacher and his artistic (art teacher) wife. The program has been tested in my classroom, and I found it to be an excellent way to make complex economic concepts come alive for my students. Not only did the students learn a good deal from running the simulation, but they also enjoyed it. (It never hurts to have students actually enjoy something you do in class!) Beverage Bonanza is shareware, IT IS NOT FREE ! If you decide to use the program, you should register it and pay the shareware fee. The cost of registering Beverage Bonanza is $5.00 for a single copy, or $20.00 for a site license. Try to get that kind of value from a commercial software company! * Macintosh and HyperCard are registered trademarks of Apple Computer, Inc.

title: SetHours XFCN 1.2.sit
description: ----------------- SetHours XFCN takes a time string and converts it to 12-hour or 24-hour format, depending on what you supply as parameter 2. If you supply 'AM' or 'PM', the XFCN returns the time in 24-hour format (e.g. SetHours('11:10','PM') returns '23:10'). If you supply 'Set', the XFCN converts a 24-hour time string to its proper 12-hour string. Version 1.2 adds an important change to the result. It now returns the result on one line instead of 2, which makes for easier scripting in most situations. You can parse for the second word of the result (e.g. '11:10 PM') instead of the second line. This version is also much smaller and faster than the previous version. Non-commercial use of this XFCN is free. =George=

title: Broker's Bailiwick 1.04Demo
description: We at QuintesSoft Custom Programming (Quintessential Software) want to invite you to discover our latest service to professional money managers anxious to offer maximum professional services with their Macs. Ignore the older Broker's Bailiwick demos in the business folder of MHC or on MBS) In simple terms, Broker's Bailiwick allows efficient day-to-day navigation through client portfolios and quick performance appraisal by seven investment sub-categories: stocks, bonds, funds, direct investments, cd's, options, and any other one category of your choice. Liberal on-screen support is provided with pop-up help fields to clarify the function and use of most buttons and fields. Among its many features, Broker's Bailiwick computes a client's true annualized rate of return, given both starting and ending dates and amounts along with the entry of all deposit and withdrawal dates and amounts. It summarizes multiple trades for each investment by dividing the sum of realized gains (or loss) by the remaining units of that investment. It concurrently requests current price(s), then computes net unrealized gain (with or without commissions) for any one investment, or any category of investments. As a user, you may merge personalized letters with addresses and salutations for mailings to selected clients, generate mailing labels, do full-feature prospect management, auto-dialing, and move prospects to active account status with assignment of an account number and a mouse click. You are also able 'sift' and sort accounts with easily-revised account marking or sorting routines (based on investment interests, funds coming due, name, city, zip, any key word or phrase). Any account or sub-account is easily converted into another sub-accounts with 4 mouse-clicks and the entry of the new sub-account number, with no re-entry of client data. Whether dealing with a prospect or current client, Each account involves over 30 buttons and 100 fields which include 32 expandable client-data fields, most of which are selectively exposed at the user's discretion. Any account can be effortlessly 'profiled' to expose only relevant fields of interest, such as funds coming due, comments, last call, last mailing, objectives, stocks to monitor, life style, topics of interest, contacts, and alternate phone numbers. Broker's Bailiwick was created under contract with the highest producing broker in his city, working for a major brokerage firm (PaineWebber). He personally solicited our company, QuintesSoft, to create something for the Mac that he was unable to find -- a program that fulfilled his concepts of astute service to his clients. It seemed such a good idea that we stipulated we would retain ownership and put much more effort into program architecture and design from the start, in anticipation of eventual broad commercial release to broker's, money managers, institutional investors, and serious private investors managing multiple accounts. Very competitive bundling is available with Apple or Outbound products, including Powerbooks and Notebooks. Additional customization and trades data file downloading is also available from QuintesSoft Custom Programming 509-779-4488, makers of QUINTESsential SOFTware for the Macintosh.

title: Deed Plotting Real Estate Land
description: ----------------- Deed Plotter 1.1 is an update of Deed Plotter 1.0. It correctly deletes invisible carriage returns from fields when the bearings are deleted. Version 1.0 did not delete carriage returns and loaded the fields up with the invisible stuff. Deed Plotter will map (to a scale that will always fit on a standard 9' Macintosh screen) any set of straight traverse lines that are entered. The resulting figure can be plotted to any desired scale, although any lines that do not appear on the screen are lost. Deed Plotter can be used to add or change traverse lines to force the traverse to close 'balancing the traverse.' The first method of closing adds an additional line which spans the gap between the beginning of the first traverse line and the end of the last traverse line. This added line represents the distance and the angle of the total error in closing the traverse. The second method of closing the polygon is by changing one of the existing lines. This method may be appropriate when existing landmarks confirm that a single gross error may have been made in the original survey description. The final method of closing the traverse distributes the total error among all the lines. The method used is known as the compass rule. This method is more appropriate when random errors have been introduced and the total error is within the predicted accuracy of the surveying techniques used. Any time the traverse is closed by one of these three methods, the area is calculated and displayed in acres.

title: Alert Manager 1.1.SIT
description: To date several programmers have written XCMDs and XFCNs that extend HyperCard by providing tools for using custom modal dialogs. Most notable of these is Jay Hodgdon's ShowDialog XCMD because it has the most features, and it is the most flexible. None, however, specifically address the use of alerts, or if they do, they are limited in some fundamental way. Consequently, I have developed, and implemented in this stack, the Alert Manager, a complete system for displaying alert dialogs using ShowDialog XCMD. Simply put, the Alert Manager allows you to call any standard-type alert dialog from a script with a minimal amount of HyperTalk code. This is accomplished through a set of handlers that control the size, appearance, and type of alert to display. Furthermore, all the necessary DLOG/DITL (dialog) templates are included to save you the trouble of hacking around in ResEdit. With the Alert Manager, all this is quite easy to implement, and even easier to handle in scripts. No conditional statements are required in your scripts to branch to the proper alert action. This is all handled by the Alert Manager scripts.
modernAnalog: Reusable confirmation/error/prompt dialog service for apps and internal tools

whyRelevant: This maps cleanly to a durable module pattern: centralized modal/alert orchestration with shared templates, response handling, and low-code invocation across many flows.

title: Tricks of the HyperTalk Masters: Polybuttons
description: Because buttons in Hpyercard are rectangular, it can be hard to use them to cover complex graphic objects with precision. When an object is irregular, slanted, elliptical, or a complex polygon, the only way to simulate a single button over it is to create a series of rectangular buttons and lay them down on the surface like tiles. You must also put the same handler in each button so that pressing each button does what you want. "Polybuttons" allows you to create buttons with multiple sides (polygons) using a special button-maker drawing tool that works like the polygon tool in Hypercard's paint layer. This stack contains the HyperTalk script for "Polybuttons" in its stack script. The script allows for the creation of free-form buttons by creating transparent rectangular buttons around an irregular shape. The useful part of the script is that it will check for overlapping buttons, thus allowing many buttons of various shapes to be in close proximity without danger of their messages being confused.
modernAnalog: interactive image-map or floorplan hotspot editor with polygon click zones

MusiCalc consists of 4 calculator elements: 1) MusiCalc, a calculator for musical time relationships (calculates time from bars & tempo, bars from time & tempo, and tempo from bars & time, and converts beats-per-minute to frames-per-beat & vice-versa); 2) TimeCalc, a time calculator which properly adds, subtracts, multiplies and divides minutes, seconds, frames, and bits (with optional entry of tenths, hundredths, etc. in place of, or combined with, these subdivisions); 3) BarCalc, a formula evaluator which simplifies computing the length of a piece of music, particularly music with changing meters and/or repeating sections whose measure-number count does not correspond to elapsed beats (BarCalc can be used for any kind of multi-part formula evaluation); and 4) SMPTECalc, which calculates sum and difference times using a stored SMPTE timecode number and MusiCalc or TimecCalc results, facilitating re-synchronization.

title: EmailLog 2.3.1 -- UPDATE
description: Use this stack to save your email correspondence and to compose email offline. Your AOL mailbox only saves messages for a limited time, so you can't just let old messages sit there indefinitely and still have them when you need them. This way you have all your correspondence at your fingertips - and you can use hypercard's find routines to locate text. EmailLog allows you to save email text files, sort and print them, find text, format text (one keystroke removes EOL's and extra spaces), compose messages offline, and open AOL with the message automatically put on the clipboard. Use EmailLog if you subscribe to any mailing lists - use a separate copy for each mailing list or correspondent, and you won't have to search through zillions of little text files to find what you are looking for. Or save all messages in one stack and sort them by correspondent. EmailLog also works for messages on FirstClass BBS's. Features include: compose multiple messages offline, select correspondents from a pop-up button, store often-used email addresses, auto-quote and auto-reply to a message, add a signature with one click. Multiple-card printing and exporting to file are supported. You can (if you choose) automatically mark for printing when importing and automatically log messages after sending; sort chronologically, or by correspondent or subject (message threads are grouped together); and import a whole folder of saved messages at once. As always, if you've been using an earlier version of EmailLog, you can import the present contents of your EmailLog stacks into a copy of this latest version. This is a very useful stack if you're carrying on any significant amount of email correspondence. If you subscribe to any mailing lists, as I do, and want to archive messages, try this out! (I have an archive of over 700 messages from one list which allows me to find and export/print old postings on a given topic within minutes.) Note: An XCMD was inadvertently not attached to version 2.3 of EmailLog. Please use this version -- 2.3.1. My apologies for any problems the defective stack caused. This stack has been checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with Virex 5.6 Previous DL count: 38

description: Personal Directory 2.0.1 is a HyperCard stack that provides you with a flexible way to access, organize, and print your personal database of names, addresses, phone numbers, and URLs. Navigation through the stack is easy, too: simply click on letter buttons to access entries via pop-up lists, and use the left and right arrow keys or buttons to move ahead or back one card. In addition, Personal Directory automatically formats phone numbers and can autodial them for you. Feel free to try out the demo version of this stack. title: MegaHome II 3.01:New Home Stack
description: MegaHome II 3.01 is a fully-documented, full-featured replacement for the HyperCard 2.x 'Home' stack. Released on January 1, 1992, it reflects exactly thirteen months' hard work on improving the stack since the release of version 1.1 on December 1, 1990. It is offered as $15 shareware from Earth Systems. MegaHome II provides numerous features not found in the Home stack shipped with HyperCard. It features a complete Finder-like interface featuring dragging, selecting and double-clicking. It also includes a moderately secure 'safe' card, known as Ft. Knox, to guard your confidential material. Another useful feature is a built-in screen-saver. All features are documented in the included MegaHome HELP stack. NOTE: Version 3.01 is functionally equivalent to version 3.0. If you are currently using version 3.0, there is NO NEED to download this archive. The only changes made in 3.01 were the addition of custom icons and the updating of the version number to reflect a final release rather than the beta-test version incorrectly indicated in 3.0. MegaHomex II's third release boasts a number of new features not found in versions 1.1 and 2.0.: -The Diagnostics stack has been incorporated into the home stack; -The confusing and virtually useless Prefs stack has been eliminated; -F15 now activates the Screen Saver, and the Screen Saver has been rewritten to resize itself to the size of the current screen, as well as other new features; -'Power Commands' are now standard; -Displays can now be temporarily hidden; -'Atheist Mode' has been added for those who do not celebrate December 25; -Script Checksums have been added to protect some code from hazardous modixcation; -User-dexnable Auto-Compacting Thresholds have been added; -A message is shown in the message box during Auto-Compacting; -A few handlers have been compiled into XCMDs and XFCNs for greater speed; -A new Intercept, compactMe (for programmers), has been added; -A 'Shrink' button has been added for users of small screens and MultiFinder or System 7; -MegaHome's menus are hidden when in other stacks; -Ft. Knox displays the stopwatch cursor properly, and security has been greatly improved; -Level 4 Support for the soon-to-be-released Module Manager has been added; -The Icon Picker is finally here--you no longer need to worry about stupid ID numbers!; -You are no longer prompted upon quitting; the quit command functions normally. 'Installing MegaHome is brief and simple. From the Finder, simply get rid of your old Home stack, and rename MegaHome, 'Home'. The next time you start up HyperCard, MegaHome will be installed.' MegaHome *must* be installed as the current Home stack. If one attempts to open the MegaHome stack when it is not installed as the current home stack, MegaHome will search for global variables that it assumed were installed upon startup time. Errors will result when it discovers that such variables were not initialized as they should have been.

You can save your conversations to a TEXT file, and read them in a word processor or in NetTalk. Unfortunately, program sharing(and maybe file sharing) need to be on for it to work.

coreUserJob: send direct network messages between people and keep a readable record of the conversation
whyRelevant: The durable job is person-to-person coordination over a local/shared network with simple transcript logging. That maps cleanly to modern internal chat, helpdesk side-channel messaging, and incident coordination modules where message history can be exported or archived.
likelyStructure: two-party or small-group text conversation interface with message send/receive, session history, and plain-text transcript save/export
searchQuerySeed: lightweight internal team chat with conversation export and transcript logging

title: DECnet NodeInfo v1.0 XFCN & Appl
description: NodeInfo v1.0 enables you to quickly view the DECnet node name area, number and executive characteristics identifier of your local node on a DECnet/Macintosh network. Included is a sample DECNet data file 'Network Data' which the NodeInfo v1.0 will attempt to interrogate. Place 'Network Data' into your System Folder in your boot drive to test the NodeInfo v1.0 software. The NodeInfo application displays a NoteAlert dialog showing the the DECnet node name, area number, node number, and DECNet Executive Characteristics Identifier. The NodeInfo XFCN returns a 4 item, comma delimited list containing the DECnet node name, area number, node number, and the DECNet Executive Characteristics Identifier. Both the NodeInfo v1.0 application and XCMD are freeware. Any comments would be welcome. Please ask me for permission to use this in commercial applications. Karl K. Nicoletti AOL: NICOLETTI Voice: 303-966-6825 (wk)

title: TOXIX 2.01 Demo
description: TOXIX provides information on toxic properties for nearly a thousand chemical substances. Each compound is characterized by name, structural formula (i.e. chemical structure), CAS (Chemical Abstracts Service) registry number, and by the type of functional groups present. Toxic properties and OSHA air exposure limits - when established - are given for each chemical substance. Hazardous properties were obtained from catalogs of chemical suppliers and from product labels and were placed in eleven different categories : known carcinogen, suspect carcinogen, teratogen, mutagen, highly toxic, toxic, irritant, lachrymator, corrosive, oxidizer, no significant hazardous properties. Exposure limits for air contaminants -current as of October 1993 - are based on the OSHA Safety and Health Standards [1900.1000 : 7-1-91 Edition].

title: ResGenie 2.2.1
description: ResGenie 2.2 is my first major revision to the original ResGenie of last year. I now support the addcolor external, full easy FX, multiple FX on opencard, new spreadhseet style windows for viewing resources, a ppat tool for easy use of ppat's with addcolor, new and improved control palettes, a new dialog for importing PICT files (allows previews-and corrects a problem where i forgot to write a renumber resource routine last time around-ouch), creates color ready stacks with superior scripts that don't blitz regular fx throughout hypercard, doesn't mess any of your menus up, doesn't take you into a 'color mode' that stops everything else. And more, much more, such as a 'link' tool that creates dynamic links between color cards and stacks--writing the correct scripts and giving you a pop up menu of color fx to choose from; a color object report tool that gives you access to all of addcolor's database info at the click of a mouse ( card or bg id's, and object ids in the case of buttons and fields). ResGenie objects no longer contain the full scripts for changing effects, etc., but still *do provide useful info* in their script headers. Includes handy 'Object' tools for listing, aligning, editing objects (a modeless real Mac window). An 'Objects' window that gives you a list of all objects, card and bg levels, lets you rename, edit contents, nudge point by point, change the visible, edit the script--all without putting you in a modal dialog (of course not). Locate hidden fields and buttons, or those dreaded '0,0,0,0' coordinate objects that you didn't even realize were there. Grab the text of locked fields without having to putter with the field tool. Get info on externals in your stacks, read the hex or ascii output from addcolor database, preview or view pictures, play sounds, record sounds (in your stack or any other file)--with more compression available than through audio palette, and more... ResGenie installs *nothing* into your home stack, doesn't work unless you tell it to, and doesn't care where it is--you push the activate button and it's ready. ResGenie adds *no* background noise to the message hierarchy (i.e., doesn't have an on openstack handler that runs every time you open any stack)... Color authoring has never been so easy or painless. Resource importing has never been so modeless. Try it. I think you'll like what you see. A small 'getting started' stack has been included to help you see the full potential of ResGenie. Import PICT files, Import resources, record sounds (any file), play sounds, get info on externals, delete resources, move resources between files, modeless operation. Create custom icons (thumbnails) for PICT and MooV files, create FX tools for addcolor, a linking tool for addcolor navigation buttons with color FX (similar to the old 'link' tool, but writes scripts for color effects as you link). Report tool for getting full addcolor database info on any color object. Much, much more. *ResGenie 2.2.1 contains some routines for helping out in low memory situations. I also included some notes on recommended RAM settings for HyperCard. Still $10. MC ------------------------ The Macintosh HyperCard Forum thanks this uploader for including Keywords at the top of the file description. For more info on uploading files and for tips on increasing the number of folks who download your files - see the document '*** Uploaders please read this' at the top of the New Files library.

title: Hanrek XCMD Shell 1.2
description: This XCMD shell is unique, in that it makes it possible for one to be immediately successful creating XCMDs in C without having to know anything about the inner workings and mechanisms of HyperCard externals. If you have, say, a function in C that you would like to call as an XCMD or XFCN, all you have to do is to open a single source file, paste the function in, copy and paste in a couple of statements and adjust them slightly (to hook up with any parameters and return result), compile it, and you're outta there! If you do not have version 7 of Symantec C, (Think C) then no problem. A HyperCard stack shows you visually the settings you need to make for a Think C project of any version. Things are structured so that you can get in and get out quickly, yet you also can get in to everything if you want. In addition, there are extensive notes to help with the commonly confusing things, such as working with handles, pointers, strings, etc.

whyRelevant: The card describes a durable integration workflow: scaffold a thin wrapper, map parameters and return values, compile, and use notes/templates to avoid low-level interop mistakes. That is a clear modern module pattern for extension SDKs, FFI wrappers, and plugin boilerplates.
likelyStructure: starter template with wrapper glue code, build/setup guidance, visual configuration help, and interop notes for parameters, return values, strings, pointers, and memory handles

title: PenPat/Mode/Size Mix & Match
description: The XFCNs in this stack demonstrate some QuickDraw techniques using CompileIt! There are a bunch of radio buttons that allow you to select the penMode and penPat to be used in the demonstration. Mix and match until you get the desired effect. The XFCNs simply draw a vertical or horizontal line of the length specified with the width, mode and pattern chosen. The line is dragged horizontally or vertically tracking to the moues. The demonstration is set up in such a manner as to emulate split bars for hyperCard fields. I specifically set up this stack to allow me to try differenct quick draw combinations on different Macs as I was experiencing results different on PowerBooks than on CRT based Macs. Gary Stanoulis CompileIt! SIG Leader

title: Volume XCMD v1.0
description: I was tired of seeing these XCMDs that let you set the volume of the Macintosh that required you to get a license to use in your own ShareWare or commericial stacks. So I made my own, and I decided to release it to anyone who wants to use it in there own stacks, royalty free! That's right, I won't ask anything for this except maybe a message to me telling that you are using it. The syntax is very simple, just type 'Volume(x).' Where 'x' is a number between 0 and 7. I haven't run into any bugs, and I assume it works on all Macintosh models. I've tested it on my LC II, an LC III, and a Power Macintosh 6100/60. I you have any comments or questions, send them to me at: BuggySoft Development 3657 Dunsmuir Cir. Pleasanton, CA 94588 USA ------------------------------------------ This archive was scanned for known viruses with Disinfectant 3.5. None were found. -------------------------------------------JGB

title: LaunchFile XCMD
description: This bug-fix release replaces the previous version dated July 19th, which had a memory leak that could cause problems if you called LaunchFile repeatedly (perhaps not until you called it hundred of times, though...). Nothing else has changed. LaunchFile is a HyperCard XCMD I wrote to provide 'more convenient' launching under System 7. I wrote it to use with PowerKey, but it will work with any program that uses HyperCard XCMDs. Give LaunchFile a pathname (full or partial) to a file, and it will do what's necessary to open the file. It can handle applications, documents, desk accessories, and aliases to all of these. The nice thing about the way documents are handled is that LaunchFile will look up the creating application; no need (or way) for you to specify it. This is supplied in a HyperCard 2.x stack - use ResEdit to extract the XCMD. Stuffit 1.5.1 archive.

title: Stack Dissector 1.1.1
description: Ooops! Never change anything trivial at the last minute. Things don't work when you do that! The help should work better in this version. Sorry for wasting people's time and money. I see over 100 people downloaded this stack in the last week or so. Wow! That's great. Thank you for showing interest. How come only one person sent me mail to tell me something was wrong with my stack? If it wasn't for Kevin I never would have known. If you don't tell me what's wrong it'll never get fixed. Please take the time to let me know what you think. Anyways back to the advertising ... Ever want to know how a stack is put together? Ever lose a button or field or handler? Ever want to tweak a stack remotely? This is the stack for you. Stack Dissector 1.1.1 is a stack browser that allows you to look through the component parts of a stack by clicking on the names of objects that interest you. Objects can be stacks, backgrounds, cards, fields, buttons, scripts or handlers. When you click on an object it expands to show the objects it contains. Shift click and you go to that object in the target stack. Option click and the dialog box for the object appears. Shareware, $10. If you like it send some mail to MSSeward. If you don't like it send some mail to Grimbeorn. Any feedback is greatly appreciated. Downloads of previous version: 138

title: Stack Dumper 2.0
description: ----------------- Stack Dumper 2.0 ================ This stack enables you to: * extract the text in the background fields of any stack * extract the scripts of any stack and then format, edit and print the fields or the scripts using MSWord Version 4. Stack Dumper generates files that can be processed through the 'Print Merge' facility of MSWord to produce fully-formatted reports from HyperCard stacks. All of MSWord's formatting facilities can be applied to produce documents from stacks. The table construction facilities of Word 4 are especially useful when printing the contents of stacks that contain multi-field cards. Stack Dumper is Freeware. You are free to use it, but I retain the copyright. If you build the Stack Dumper scripts into any of your stacks, give me and my university an acknowledgement. If you want to use any of my code in a product, contact me for a licence. I hope you find 'Stack Dumper' useful. Please e-mail me if you like it. Let me know about any problems or improvements that you discover too. A primitive version of this stack, called Stack Dumper 1.0 was released in September 1989. That version could only dump background fields, not scripts, and you were required to copy its scripts into the target stack before you could use the dumping facilities. title: Vocabulary Situations Lite
description: 'Vocabulary Situations Lite' Phil Shapiro posted a text file in this area in February requesting that someone build a stack around it. He described the file as follows: 'This short text file is a series of 'vocabulary situations' ...' '... Each vocabulary situation describes a scenario where students are called upon to choose from one of two options. To choose the correct option, though, students needs to understand the vocabulary used in describing the scenario.' This stack is my response to his request. The stack contains the thirty situations in the list, has room for up to 170 more, and the stack can be adapted for other flash card purposes. See the Help stack for instructions on how to edit the data. A version containing more sound resources was uploaded to AOL earlier. Since the size of the stack with all the extra data might have been off-putting to potential downloaders (there have been only 17 downloads to date - we should have named it Naked Girl Situations), I split the stack and put the sounds and some utilities in a stack called 'VS Sounds'. You might call this version 'Vocabulary Situations Lite'. The functionality of the stack is the same, except that it looks for the stack 'VS Sounds' and starts using the sounds in that stack for transitions. If it doesn't find it the first time, it never looks again. You don't need 'VS Sounds' unless you want a larger variety of sounds or you want to easily record or scrounge or manipulate the sounds to your own taste. As always, response is requested. Fillmore 051695

title: PowerList 2.0
description: ----------------- First off, let me apologize for the size of this stack. As you will see, I tried extra hard to explain every possible detail about this new version so I ended up with quite a large stack. In addition to that, I also included a PowerList installer XCMD to assist you in moving PowerList among your various stacks. PowerList is a XFCN that brings up a dialog box containing a scrollable list of information stored in a container. PowerList includes several features missing in other similar externals. PowerList has the ability to accept input by line (return-delimited), by item (comma-delimited), by STR or STR# resource, or by any text file. PowerList will optionally sort the list before displaying it in the dialog. PowerList accepts several different 'parameters' which determine varying selection criteria of the list. It will also accept a prompt string for display in its dialog box. PowerList allows you change the font of the dialog box to any currently available font, and the dialog itself can be placed anywhere on the screen. PowerList 2.0 introduces a new and exciting trend in how the user determines all of the available features operate and how the dialog box looks on the screen. The user now supplies a 'configuration string' to PowerList that contains several 'keywords' that determine the exact function of each individual feature. And the greatest of all features, Cloning liscenses!

title: Kaleidagraph AppleEvent Scrip...
description: Kaleidagraph AppleEvent Scrip... Sunday, June 21, 1998 The file is a self extracting archive containing two stacks, one for scripting and one to provide online scripting help, and a documentation file in MS Word format. The scripting stack contains the SendAppleEvent XCMD written by Donald Olsen of Apple Computer, which enable HC 2.1 to send AppleEvents of arbitrary class and ID to any AppleEvent-aware program. The stacks have been set up to work with and provide online help for the scripting of Kaleidagraph by Synergy Software, which in version 3.0 adds AppleEvent support to a fine program for interactive data visualization and analysis. The stacks can be used with Kaleidagraph 3.0 without any detailed knowledge of AppleEvents, but a knowledge of Kaleidagraph internal scripting is needed, since AppleEvents are used simply to send text scripts to Kaleidagraph. The help stack documents all scripting features of KG3, and buttons are provided to access the Kaleidagraph general help utility. These stacks were derived from similar stacks which were distributed with Spyglass Transform 2.1. It should be fairly straightforward to adapt them to any AppleEvent-aware program to accomplish bulk file processing, given documentation of the AppleEvents understood by the target program and a working knowledge of HyperCard scripting.

title: TimeSpent XFCN
description: ----------------- TimeSpent XFCN calculates elapsed time between two time parameters. Extremely flexible, the function allows you to include or leave out the 'am/pm' designator, or to use 24-hour time formats. Your choice. The XFCN will correctly calculate over the 12-hour delineators. You can also format the result in any of four ways, including real number for calculating by hourly rates, or three formats in separate lines, so you can place one in a field and use another for calculations. Version 1.2 fixes a bug that would provide incorrect results when the end time was between 12:00 and 12:59, if the 'am' or 'pm' designator was included. Use of this XFCN is free in non-commercial stacks if credit is given. Copyright 1990 by George Pytlik. All rights reserved.

title: ChangeStackFont 1.11
description: ChangeStackFont 1.11/Feb. 22, 1992 *Version 1.11 fixes a functional bug that would not display the correct font info for a field at startup, if you had previously removed a font used by the stack since last launching it. INTRO This stack is a by-product of some scripted font manipulation techniques I developed for a couple of different stacks I've recently created. It does two things: First - it counts your installed fonts, and allows you change this stack's font and font styles, so you can view/browse your fonts. Second - the idea here is to give the user a bit of flexibility in how a stack is displayed, in that the functionality of ChangeStackFont can be moved to other stacks, if you follow the three steps noted in the stack...a full description of the scripting technique is included. USE IT IN YOUR STACK! To move the functionality of this stack into another stack, copy the things noted to your stack (adjust scripts to allow for the way I update sample text fields in my stack.) And that's it. THE FUTURE: Someday (perhaps) I might update the idea of changeStackFont into a single object that can be more easily moved to other stacks, if there's enough interest. In the meantime, if you distribute this stack, please include this Read Me document. And, if you like this stack, well...great! Drop me a line and let me know how you like it or how you've used it! ChangeStackFont by Bill Underwood, 1/5/92 AppleLink: UNDERWOOD.B AOL: ZenZa (or) QuickTime

title: Progress XCMD 2.0r
description: Progress XCMD 2.0r Sunday, June 21, 1998 Up until now, the only method of providing visual feed-back to the user that HyperCard has NOT hung is to do such things as animate the cursor or plug 'update' comments into the message box during the execution of time-consuming scripts. title: HCSC 052295 Text-to-Speech
description: The enclosed stacks and documents were part of a package that was issued in connection with the 5/22/95 America Online HyperCard Forum Scripting Conference. The HyperCard Scripting Conference is simply an ongoing series of online meetings in the AOL HyperCard conference area (Shortcut: MHC), usually held on Monday evenings. Some of these meetings have formal topics with guest presenters, others are informal get togethers where HyperCard scripting enthusiasts can ask and answer questions on any topic as well as share scripts and tips. Anyone with an AOL account can attend. The subject of the 5/22/95 conference was HyperCard and the text-to-speech capabilities provided by the Speech Manager Extension. Although America Online provides the venue for the conferences, it is in no way responsible for them and does not, in any way, own or endorse the contents of the discussions or the associated stacks. Since the conference there have been a number of changes to the Speech Manager. As of this writing, we are up to version 1.4.1. I have not had a chance to do a comprehensive check of the behavior of the latest versions, so I can't guarantee that the notes about bugs and quirks remain accurate. However, the scripting suggestions and the guide to the use of inline commands and the phonemic symbols should remain generally useful. There are some important differences between the versions of the Speech Manager that were available when the stack was created and the latest version. First of all, it is now possible to set the default voice using the Speech control panel. When the stack was created, the default was restricted to the voice "Fred". The examples in the stack will probably work better if you set the default to "Fred". Since there is no longer a built in default voice or a built in synthesizer, you must, contrary to the information in the stack, have at least one voice installed in the Voices folder in the Extensions folder as well as a synthesizer for text-to-speech to work. Another important difference is that there are now three Speech synthesizers available, Macintalk II, Macintalk III, and Macintalk Pro. Few HyperCard scripters realize that is is possible to embed special commands into text that is to be spoken. These commands allow one to have much greater control over how the synthesizer "speaks" the text. Perhaps the greatest value of this stack is that it documents this feature. Before closing this note, I would especially like to thank to Jeff Crossley, who organized the HyperCard Scripting Conference, and who authored the template for the Script Conference demo stacks. Mark Klink

title: Mental Giant
description: Mental Giant is a HyperCard stack which develops one's ability to perform mental math. Click the 'Begin' button to begin (what else?). After determining your choice of either 'Silent' or 'Speaking' version, your gender, and asking your name, Mental Giant will then ask a series of twenty-five mental math problems. Type in the answer when presented with a dialog box and either push return, enter, or click OK. Players are timed how long it takes them to finish. The smaller the amount of time it takes a player to finish, the higher his or her standing in the 'Masters' listing; only the lowest ten are listed. (Hint: the silent version runs a little faster). You may check the listings before you begin by clicking the 'Scores' button. These listings will also be shown at the end should your score be listed. A 'thermometer' (Mental Measure) along the right hand side of the card registers your progress. The rest is practice, and a little 'evolution.' If you enjoyed my stack, 'The House of Multiplication Horror,' you will enjoy this stack as well.

title: HTML Optimizer 3.0.3
description: HTML Optimizer offers FOUR functions: 1. HTML Optimizer saves 10 to 15% of space on your web pages by removing unnecessary characters and tags. Though this may seem a small improvement, load time savings is much higher as your client's Internet browser parses the pages more efficient. Embedded script code is not ruined by HTML Optimizer! Your original pages remain untouched for later updating. New text files are created with same Creator as original and are put in a folder named "Optimizer Output". Take care that you do not replace your original HTML pages with optimized ones because you may lose your nicely indented layout. Keep the originals in a safe place. 2. Another useful function of HTML Optimizer is checking the socalled tagged pairs, i.e. the tags that always have to appear together with their end tags, for example .... All dangling tags are reported. Further, the IMG tags are checked for the presence of the Width, Height and Alt attributes. The Width and Height attributes help the browser to build up the page faster because it can reserve the necessary room for the image while placing the text. The Alt attributes make your pages more user-friendly. 3. The Mark function does the same as the Check function, but produces new files with the incomplete tags marked with the character •, so that you can easily find them for fixing. 4. The Convert function takes care that special characters between ASCII 128 and 255 are converted to the appropriate &.....; notation.
modernAnalog: static site HTML lint-and-minify preflight tool
coreUserJob: optimize and validate HTML files before publishing so pages load efficiently and markup issues are easier to fix
whyRelevant: The card maps cleanly to a durable publishing workflow: generate a safe output copy, reduce payload size, validate required markup pairs and attributes, flag issues inline, and normalize character encoding. title: Script Optimizer 1.0.1
description: Script Optimizer 1.0.1 is a HyperCard stack that can analyze other stacks' scripts and perform one to three optimizations on them: * keyword abbrevation * comment removal * global checking Keyword Abbreviation HyperCardUs long-format keywords, such as 'background' can be abbreviated to their shortest equivalent, such as 'bg.' (Any keywords within quotation marks are left as-is.) Comment Removal Removes lines that begin with '--', and 'embedded comments' within a line, such as 'on mouse up -- user stops'. (Comment characters inside quotation marks are not removed.) Global Checking Often during the development cycle of a stack, some global variables remain within handlers, even though they are not used any longer. These unused globals can be removed. (Global variables that are declared within quotation marks are not checked.) Script Optimizer also generates detailed reports on all optimization that it makes to a stack.

title: Pediatric Diagnosis #2(PEDDX2...
description: These Hypercard stacks require Hypercard v. 2 and because of their size must be run from either a hard disk or a 1.4 MB floppy drive. The hard disk is the preferred storage medium since they run v-e-r-y s-l-o-w-l-y on a floppy drive. This is the second of a series of interactive Hypercard stacks designed to test one's ability to approach the diagnosis of a pediatric patient's illness in a logical and thoughtful fashion. The stacks are designed so as to be as close to 'real life' as is possible, including the element of time. You are given the history of three patients' illnesses and pertinent physical findings. After that you may request those laboratory studies you deem necessary until you feel certain of the diagnosis and proper therapy for each patient. The 'grades' you receive are based upon the cost of the studies and hospitalization (if needed) to the patient; it is hoped that this method will give the 'testee' an appreciation of the high cost of medical care and the necessity for being thoughful in the way he/she orders laboratory studies. The denouement of each case discusses the differential diagnosis as well as a logical approach to the diagnosis. Each case has with it a current (1992) bibliography. The stacks are designed for medical students, house officers and physicians but may be of interest to others. Clare N. Shumway, M.D. (Clarens)
modernAnalog: case-based pediatric diagnostic reasoning simulator with cost-aware test ordering
coreUserJob: practice diagnosing pediatric cases by choosing history, exams, and lab work while balancing certainty, treatment choice, and resource use

title: Cyphercard 1.0a
description: Cyphercard is a simple but effective tool (a Hypercard stack) for encoding and decoding text documents. It uses no complex math, but simply encodes one document (the target) by using a second document as a reference document or a 'one time scratch pad', as they call it in the cypher biz. Cyphercard does not just use simple pointers into the reference document, but modulates those pointers using offsets which are also taken from that document. It uses various other encoding techniques as well. None of this is complicated or tricky. It doesn't need to be. The security of the code is not compromised at all by knowing exactly how it was coded. All the security comes from the fact that the code depends on the reference document and the password. The upshot is that anyone who knows which reference document and password to use can decode any document that was encoded with that reference document and password. Anyone who does not know that document and password will not be able to decode the target document. Cyphercard requires a PowerPC Macintosh and Hypercard 2.4.
modernAnalog: shared-secret text file encoder/decoder for controlled message exchange
coreUserJob: encode a text document so only someone with the same reference text and password can decode it
whyRelevant: The durable job is secure text exchange based on a shared secret and reversible transform. That still maps to lightweight message protection, though the described method is niche and weaker as a reusable modern module than standard secret-sharing or encrypted-note flows.
likelyStructure: form-based utility with plaintext input, shared secret/reference input, password field, encode/decode action, and output panel

Click on the Start button and you will be brought to the Sentence Production card (Fig. 1). Click the Produce a Sentence button. On the screen you will first see the sentence pattern which was randomly chosen from the Sentence Pattern card (Fig. 2). Next, you will see the words or phrases to be replaced (wildcards) followed by the words or phrases (substitutions) randomly chosen from their respective cards (Fig. 3) which will replace the wildcards Finally, the finished sentence will be displayed on the screen. If you have chosen the Macintalk option and if you have Macintalk in your system folder, you will hear the sentence spoken by Macintosh's speech synthesizer. From: http://iteslj.org/Articles/KellyRSdoc.html

title: AppleEvent XCMDs
description: This stack contains a collection of Apple Event™ XCMDs and handlers which can be used to send Apple events to Apple event-aware applications from HyperCard. It replaces the SendAppleEvent XCMD stack which has been previously distributed via AppleLink and Apple's Developer CD. Object specifiers and AERecords are now supported in this stack! With this HyperCard stack, you may easily create and send Apple events to an application. By reading an application's 'aete' resource, which generates glue routines, you can "test drive" the application by sending it events that it supports. Essentially, this is a mini development environment for scripting other applications using HyperTalk. Since it is entirely implemented in HyperTalk and HyperCard, it may be a little slower for the low-end machines, but nevertheless, it is a rich environment in which new features can easily be added.

title: TextMuncher 0.93
description: From the Help field: TextMuncher is a text processor. I created it because I found that I sometimes needed to edit or format text in ways not easy in a word processor. TextMuncher not only cleans up raw ASCII text, but it also allows you to do such things as: change all words which begin with 'pre' to upper case, or delete any lines which contain 'http://', or set each word which immediately precedes 'Instructions:' to a given textFont, textSize, and textStyle. Capabilities: *** Operations: clean up ASCII text by taking out EOL's, replacing double en-dashes with em-dashes, smart-quoting, eliminating superfluous hyphens, etc. Also delete, replace, sort, change case, set text styles, and extract specified chunks of text to the clipboard. *** Operate on a selected block of text or on all lines/words/characters/strings which contain/don't contain/equal/begin with/end with/follow/precede a specified string, in the entire text or in any selected block of text. *** Define any combination of textFont, textSize, and textStyle as a named style, and apply it, via a pop-up list, to selected text or to all lines/words/... etc., which contain/don't contain/... etc. Like a limited version of word-processor styling, but with much more flexibility in applying styles automatically to specified pieces of text. *** Undo any of the above operations. *** Export the formatted text as an RTF file, which can be opened by most word processors. Limitations: *** Only works (for now) on text files less than 30K. *** Very slow on large searches. (That's Hypercard - powerful but slow.) *** Somewhat fussy about its operations. However, for certain procedures, it's still lots faster than doing it by hand in a word processor! DL this stack and keep it on hand. If in two months you haven't used it, then trash it. But I bet in that time you'll have come across at least one situation when you'll find it very handy...

title: MerryXmas Virus
description: MerryXmas was the first documented HyperCard virus, and was the only prevalent HyperCard virus for many years. When an infected stack is opened, the virus script copies itself to the Home stack. From there, it spreads to every other stack that is opened. Unopened stacks are not affected, but it still does not take long for most of the stacks on a hard drive to contract the virus. The original intent of MerryXmas was to simply propagate, but because of a typing error, the virus can sometimes copy parts of other stack scripts along with its own virus code. This causes stacks to behave unexpectedly or sometimes to error. In certain circumstances, MerryXmas also causes HyperCard to quit (but not crash) without any notification. (from https://www.hyperactivesw.com/hypercard/virus1.html ) This stack includes both a stack to infect the Home stack (in the disk 'Virus') and the tool 'MerryXmas Vaccine' (in the disk 'disk') that can detect and disinfect the virus. Please note that the emulator is an isolated environment and therefore all changes (and infected stacks) are lost when the emulator is closed. The came cannot be said if you download this stack to run elsewhere - extreme caution is advised! Particular thanks to Space Rogue of The Whacked Mac Archives for tracking down this virus so it can be preserved in the HyperCard collection.

coreUserJob: Detect self-propagating script contamination in shared files, identify affected assets, and disinfect them before further spread or breakage
whyRelevant: The durable job is not the legacy platform but propagation control, integrity checking, and remediation of reusable assets that infect other assets when opened or run. The card also implies a compact module shape: inspect, detect infection pattern, report affected items, and clean them.

title: HyperSavvy HOT for scripters v1.
description: Many of the scripts in HyperSavvy have already proven themselves out in the field, in other stacks, enjoying a minimum of a year's worth of debugging and polishing before ever appearing in HyperSavvy; so it's highly unlikely you'll encounter a problem with them. This stack is NOT PROTECTED, and is set to USERLEVEL 5. So you have full access to everything (so long as you possess the full-blown HyperCard application). This allows you to use HyperSavvy as a 'template' stack- transform it directly into your own custom stacks- or borrow bits and pieces instead, as preferred. Anyone may use these scripts (AND the custom navigation palette) in their own shareware and commercial stacks, with no more obligation than plainly stating within those stacks the source of the code and palette. Major scripting techniques presented in HyperSavvy (there's lots of minor ones not listed here) include the following: 1. Central control over stack navigation that ties together the cursor/arrow keys, Go menu commands, custom menu commands, keyboard menu shortcuts, and an attractive custom navigation palette into a single system. This allows you to change all stack navigation operations with just the tweak of a few central script handlers. 2. Your own 'About [filename]...' under the Apple menu, and 'Quit [filename]' under the File menu, and a custom '[filename]' menu, easily customized within MINUTES for your own stacks. 3. Comprehensive modifications of HyperCard menus on demand- menu AND menu item deletions, disablings, enablings, breaks, and redirections of commands- whatever you require for your own project. 4. A fast and elegant PopUp menu (in pure HyperTalk) which changes 'on-the-fly' to present different options for different cards. 5. An exhaustive trap script for all HyperCard menu calls- simply insert your own custom scripts for any menu item you desire. This gives you absolute control of every menu command in a stack (and so is great when you want to protect your stack from hackers with a few special tricks of your own). 6. Full custom menu and palette wrap, and transparent userLevel changes, within multi-stack environments. This greatly reduces the chance of script errors, while also making your own stacks behave much more professionally under complex conditions. You (and your stack's users) will be delighted. 7. A wide range of ask and answer dialog boxes, from simple to complex. 8. Three different styles of progress gauge dialogs, including the standard bar, and new choices of page and circular styles, all equipped with Abort buttons (pure HyperTalk). 9. A versatile scrolling list dialog with click-to-choose and multiple function buttons. 10. 'Find...' and 'Find Next' scripts complete with a full range of contingency dialogs already built-in, ready and waiting to be pasted into your own stack. 11. Background processing for HyperCard, with examples providing time and date readouts for users, and a trap script that'll tell you everytime a user begins or quits typing into a field. 12. Ways to keep tabs on the size of any script or text field in a stack, and intercept and deal with HyperCard script errors in a way invisible to end-users. Why spend months trying to gather up and comprehend all these techniques separately, with little or no help, and then spend more months attempting to make them all work together, when you can just download HyperSavvy today? Become an overnight expert in HyperCard- by downloading HyperSavvy HOT today.

title: HyperGrapher Calculator 1.0b
description: TYPE: Freeware Stack SYSTEM 7: Compatible Author's email address: bscott@east.east-slc.edu I wrote this simple graphic calculator stack because I couldn't afford Mathematica, nor could I buy one of those spiffy PowerMacintoshes with the new 3D graphic calculator on it. So, for those of you who already have one of the above items, then you don't need this stack. However, if you're just an average student-user like myself, read on. Some helpful hints: 1: Type the equations into the fields in HyperCard's standard form. X3 would be typed 'X^3' (minus the apostrophes). 2: All odd exponent powers are rounded off (absolute value)x To make up for this, multiply the variable times itself raised to one less power. 3: The four small fields control the setting of the X-axis and Y-axis, much like the RANGE values found in graphic calculators. The X-off and Y-off are the offset, or how many points (not pixels) to the right, left, up, or down of the origin (the REAL origin). The Xscl and Yscl variables set how many pixels are to be places between each point on the grid. So, if you wanted to 'zoom in', you'd enter in a big number. If you wanted to zoom out, you'd enter in a small number (at least 1). It's not exactly the best screen to zoom in and out of, but it's bigger than some of those hand-held calculator screens, and better resolution too! 4: The Equation Field is merely a field I created to store all my conic section formulas (solved for x). You can modify or copy from it any way you'd like, and I hope you DO personalize it. (Just make sure you put the previous equations BACK if you distribute your working copy.)

title: PickupBackground XCMD
description: ----------------- HyperCard 2 format but converted from original HyperCard 1.2.5 stack. WHAT IS IT? When are backgrounds the same? Ever get frustrated when copying a card from one place to another, a card which supposedly has the 'same' background, only to find that a new background was created? HyperCard has a quite literal view of when backgrounds are the same, namely, when their byte counts & such are identical. This XCMD will let you paste card-specific information (card picture, field text, card fields, card buttons and card script) 'into' another background. In effect this lets you change which background a certain card belongs to. Extremely useful in merging multiple stacks or backgrounds together. USAGE 1) Go to the card you wish to move to a different background. 2) Choose 'Copy Card' (or 'Cut Card') from the Edit menu. 3) Go to the background which you want the copied card to belong to. 4) Hold down the option key while selecting 'Paste Card' from the Edit menu. or Call the XCMD directly as explained in the notes.

title: MiniTool Palette
description: The MiniTool is a small 2 1/2' by 1/2' palette with nine icons. Combining modifier keys with single clicks allows you to perform over 50 different navigation and authoring tasks. The palette is completely 'self-contained' and requires no additional stack scripting; just copy its three resources into your Home stack, type 'palette miniTool' in the Message Box, and away you go. Using combinations of modifier keys and single clicks, MiniTool can take you to the 1st card, previous card, recent card, 1st bkgnd, previous bkgnd, next card, last card, next bkgnd, last bkgnd, or any stack window. It can open a stack, open the Recent display, push a card, pop a card, start using a stack, stop using a stack, close open windows, hide all HyperCard windows, open ResCopy, open ResEdit, open any other application, open any stack's script, rush you Home, open Home's script, call GetHomeInfo, and do several other things. Stealth mode lets you set lockMessages to true via a modifier key, while you click any palette icon; a real lifesaver when a resumeStack or openCard handler has run amok, or when you're checking out someone else's stack and want to avoid transition effect scripts, 'security' handlers, etc. Of course, if you don't mind adding about five lines of code to your Home stack script, you can also use the MiniShow XCMD, which keeps the palette 'pinned' above the center of the current stack. (Of course, it's smart enough to take the title bar and menu bar into account, and it never lets the palette become lost chasing off-screen card window locations). Complete documentation is in the stack, and the palette displays a comprehensive Help window with a single click. The stack, palette, and MiniShow external require HC 2.0 or higher. A couple of bonus externals that work with HC 1.2.2 or higher are also included. Grokware -- if you 'get' it, you can keep it.

title: Softcourse System 1.22 BETA
description: Shareware, $20 Softcourse was originally designed to assist college-level students in learning information presented to them through video telecourses. It affords these students an opportunity to experience a more structured learning environment (which the television courses lacked) without confining a student to a set class schedule or instructor. The technology of television freed the student from having to attend lectures while allowing them to still see them; Computer technology allows the student a more involved environment for learning things with the same flexibility that the television course material offers. Softcourse is a HyperCard stack that is already half-written for you. This half-written stack is laid out in a way so that any information put into it will be in an accessible and learnable format. All of the heavy technical-whizbang stuff has been figured out for you. Softcourse is meant to let you focus on what you do the best: sharing the knowledge you have. In many ways, it is like a form which you can fill your very own information into, doodle a little here and there, and feed it through a machine that makes it a finished product. You are, of course, still very responsible for the content of your material, but the stress of presenting that material in this new way (on the computer) is lessened. The reason for that is (hopefully) because you don't have to worry about much else. What Softcourse offers to you, the instructor, is the freedom to construct a piece of information-rich courseware that not only allows the student to engage in active, self-directed exploration of material, but also automatically remediates. That is, it alters how it presents material to the student as it finds out what portions of the material they are having difficulty with, on the fly. Many courseware program force learners to absorb the material in an almost linear fashion, and become limiting, binding, and impractical. Softcourse attempts to be different. It provides you the opportunity to positively utilize HyperCard without learning all of its intracies. On the other hand, the more you learn about HyperCard, the more you can make your courseware interactive and customized. You want to have a certain piece of material jump to another stack, do something, and jump back? Softcourse allows you to modify and add to any portion of it, until your courseware is just right. In essence, it lets you concentrate on the content of a stack, while Softcourse software concetrates on the function. From that end, your students get your best material, a step-by-step list of things to explore, structured tests set up to your specifications, auto-remediation, and it still lets them access any piece of information, look at any test's results, take any test, or check what their list of things to do it at any time. Pretty Spiffy, eh? Megs

title: Proportion Wheel 2.0
description: Proportion Wheel allows the user to properly scale photos for publication. It doesn't matter whether you use picas, inches or even mixed measures --although this version will not convert picas to inches. To use Proportion Wheel, press the New Photo button. It will ask you the original width, new width, original length and new length of the photo you are measuring. It is important that you fill in only three of the four required numbers. To determine the percentage and either new length or width of photo, press the Go Figure button. If you filled in all four blanks, Go Figure will put a new number in the New Width box. To change a single number in the Width or Length boxes (you can change one without affecting the other numbers), simply click on the one you want to change, enter the new number and press Go Figure. Remember that before you press the Go Figure button, one of the four Width and Length boxes must be empty. To convert fractions to their decimal equivalent, or vice-versa, click on Converter and enter either the fraction or the decimal. You can convert fractions as small as 1/64th of an inch. To get rid of the Converter box, simply click on it.
modernAnalog: image resize and print-layout scaling calculator

title: Timed-Out v1.11
description: Version1.11.....January 11, 1993 ___________________ Even though HyperCard is a handy, simple-to-work-with database, it cannot be shared by multiple users on a network. When the standard System 7 sharing is used, I believe a stack open on one computer can be accessed as read only by another computer. Worse yet, using Appleshare on a server, a person trying to access a stack on the server that is already in use gets a message something like 'This stack is busy.' I hope this can be remedied in the next update of HyperCard. One way to help work around this problem, when you need to have multiple stack users, is to have the database stack automatically quit afer a defined period of time. This stack contains a simple script at the stack level to do just that. After 5 minutes, it will show a hidden field stating...... 'If you need another 5 minutes use-time, hit the 'Reset Use-time' button, otherwise this stack will quit in 1 minute to make it available for other users.' The stack's 'Reset Use-time' button can be used to respond to or preempt the inevitable warning. Copy the script, button, and hidden background field (named 'Warning') to your HyperCard database stack, and change the timing to whatever is reasonable for typical use of your database. If you have other solutions, I think a lot of Mac users would be appreciative. Robert Hangebrauck...RobertPH on American Online....Internet: robertph@aol.com
modernAnalog: shared workstation or app session timeout with warning and extend-session prompt
coreUserJob: keep a limited shared database or tool available by automatically ending idle or overlong sessions unless the current user explicitly extends access
whyRelevant: This maps cleanly to durable access-control and resource-availability workflows: session expiration, idle timeout warnings, and user-driven extension before forced release. The module shape is reusable for shared terminals, licensed tools, kiosks, and constrained internal systems.

title: TagNotes 1.0
description: Text is entered into the main text field. Each card's tags appear in the 'tags' field. A running list of all tags attached to all cards appears in the 'all tags' field. Tags can be created, attached to a card, detached from a card, or deleted from the stack entirely. Any tag can be made the 'active tag.' It is easy to navigate to the next card bearing the active tag, to search for the active tag in the text of any or all cards, and to export the text of the cards with the active tag. In this way, multiple connections may be made between topics, subtopics, related topics, etc. and information may be organized and accessed according to the needs of the moment. If you're a student and you use a PowerBook, try taking lecture notes or reading notes in this stack. If you then attach appropriate tags to the cards, you'll be able to use the tags and searching capabilities to review your notes in a very flexible way. Or if you have a whole bunch of information on some general topic, and you want a way to access it easily, try importing it into TagNotes.

title: Scroll from the keyboard
description: The card scripts in this stack enable you to scroll text in a specified field from the keyboard. The 1st card uses the Up and Down arrow keys, while the next card is geared toward older Macs that don't have arrow keys. You can retain at least some of the quick-look aspect by enabling users to scroll text with a single key. The up and down arrow keys used alone scroll text by 'screenfuls' the amount of text visible at any one time in the field. Pressing Option-Up arrow takes you back to the beginning of the text, and pressing Shift-Down arrow or Shift-Up arrow scrolls text down or up by one line. The script enables the scrolling by measuring the height of the field and the height of text in the field, and by calculating how much text is scrolled 'off the top' of the field. It seems to work well with any field, but try to size your fields so that they show full lines of text. If you size a field so that the bottom line is half hidden, for instance, you might not get consistent scrolling.
modernAnalog: Keyboard-driven scrolling for a text viewer, notes pane, log panel, or record detail field

title: Scientific Bibliography Stack...
description: OPTIONAL: Access to SilverPlatter Medline SYSTEM 7: Compatible TYPE: Freeware stack Author's email address: lho@biobase.aau.dk Author's description: This stack is my first go at making a HyperCard stack. I have written it, because I needed a database for my scientific articles. The stack may also be used to keep a database of your books, etc. I have written an import function in the stack, this makes it possible to import downloaded from Silverplatter Medline. If you know a little about 'programming' in HyperCard, it is very easy to make it import other formats. Feel free to use this stack and give copies away, but don't charge money for it!!! You may also modify your personal copy, customising it to your needs. Please give the original copy to other interested Mac users. Input: You can either type the references into the correct field yourself or use the import-function. When typing the references yourself, the tab key will move the insertion point to the next field. The import-function is designed for Silverplatter Medline. You will find the function in the menu bar under 'x' (sigma). Each field, in a reference to be imported, is separated by a return and each new reference is separated by three returns. Further help on the format to be found in the stack. I have included a file downloaded from Medline, where you can see the format. You can also try importing this file with the import-function (it should work). If you want to import text from another database, it is very easy to modify the script yourself. If you are not familiar with HyperCard scripting, you are welcome to drop me a line. Output: You can transfer the references to a text file with the export-function in the menu bar under 'x' (sigma). Each field will be separated by a tab and each reference with a return. You can of course also modify this in any way you may like. I think this is very usefull for generating a bibliogaphy, if you are writing articles yourself. You can also print the references, just like any other HyperCard stack, by choosing 'Print report' from the menu bar.

title: About XCMD 1.1
description: About XCMD 1.1 Sunday, June 21, 1998 If you knock yourself out creating a stack that is rich in features and possessing of great depth, you begin to think of your stack as an application, with HyperCard being the 'engine' that drives it. If that is the case then you should be able to give your stack credit by placing 'About xxxx...' under the Apple in the same fashion as is done by all applications. The arguments of About XCMD are: Pass one parameter: About 'About this stack...' This replaces the 'About HyperCard...' menu item with one of your own. Pass two parameters: About 'About this stack...','Stack Help...' This also adds a second custom menu choice just below the 'About...' item. If you do not wish to change the 'About HyperCard...' item then pass a SPACE as the first parameter: About ' ','Stack help...' -- remember to pass a SPACE as the 1st parameter! Pass NO parameters: About This restores the Apple menu to its original state. It doesn't matter whether you customized the menu with one or two items. Use this form in your closeStack handler. This stack includes the source code to the XCMD.

title: Xtrace/Xtrap.sit
description: ----------------- XTrace and XTrap are XCMD/XFCN debugging utilities. They can be used to see what callbacks an XCMD/XFCN is making into HyperCard, and to change the results returned to the XCMD/XFCN. (Throughout this text XCMDs and XFCNs are referred to simply as XCMDs. XTrace and XTrap work with either). >>XTrace description XTrace loads and executes any XCMD and traps the callbacks the XCMD makes to HyperCard. It builds a list of the callbacks and their parameters, and puts the list into the HyperTalk global variable 'callbacks.' This list can be displayed to show what callbacks the XCMD made. The callbacks are executed, and the behavior of the XCMD is not affected. >>XTrap description XTrap is similar to XTrace, but rather than making a list of the callbacks, it sends a HyperTalk message to the current card with the callback information, and lets you write a handler to intercept the callback and even change the value returned. For example, suppose you use an XCMD that gets the name of the current stack and prints the stack name on a report, and you'd like it to print something else. You can trap the callback that asks for the stack name [EvalExpr('the short name of this stack')] and return something else. After using XTrace to see what callbacks an XCMD makes, you can use XTrap to change the results and modify the XCMD's behavior (or get more control over what the XCMD does to your stack).

likelyStructure: wrapper that executes an extension, records outbound host callbacks with arguments, and optionally routes each callback through a user-defined handler that can pass through or replace the return value

title: CharToHex and HexToChar XFCNs
description: This stack contains two HyperCard XFCNs. The CharToHex XFCN returns the hexadecimal value of an ASCII character passed to it. The HexToChar XFCN returns the ASCII character equivalent of a hexadecimal value passed to it. Both XFCNs have an additional option to return the decimal equivalent also. This means that the a single line of script can return the hex and decimal values of an ASCII character. e.g. 1 put CharToHex(A) -- returns 41 e.g. 2 put CharToHex(A,true) -- returns 41,65 This stack shows a simple example of their use. The CharToHex XFCN and the HexToChar XFCN are Copyright 1992 - Barry Dobson - workingweekend productions. These externals are freeware for non-commercial use and may be used in any non-commercial stack. Please feel free to contact me with any comments. I can be reached via AOL screen name Quoin.

title: Scavenger Hunt
description: WELCOME TO SCAVENGER HUNTÂ©! This software was created with children in mind. The program is specifically created for kindergarten through second graders; those who are working on reading skills. It not only offers reading and matching exercises throughout the basic game, but each room of the house has a "hidden" activity that helps underline reading disciplines: â¢ in one room, children play "dress up" and learn clothing names â¢ in another room, children follow a recipe to create a new treat â¢ another room offers a book that keeps stealing letters from a story â¢ in other rooms, there is opportunity to match tools, fold laundry, and try out interesting pets â¢ and other rooms offer fun sounds, games and other spelling feats! Of course, we can't tell you where any of these rooms are, nor how to get into them, that's part of the fun! The basic "game" as you enter the house is to go from room to room using the "map" provided on the main page. Try to find objects that BEGIN with the letters on your "list" found with the map. You will only be able to take one object back with you from any room, and once you make a letter match, that room will be "locked." (This feature can be turned off if you wish, by clicking "off" the button "Use room covers" below the house map.) You have to find ONE object for each letter to complete the game, and there are literally HUNDREDS of objects all over the house!! If you wish to remove a word you found, click on that word in your list and it will be removed (and if you are playing with room covers, the room will be unlocked so you can go back and find other options.) You may want to "explore" before you begin, so you have a feel for where things are. The game will ONLY speak the first letters, not the entire word. This encourages children and adults to work together and "read" the game. There are fun side tracks all through the house. Check doors, cupboards and piles of things, you never know what you'll find! But most of all, have fun!! If your version is titled "MiniScavenger HuntÂ©", then your program is made to fit on the MacClassic screen (9"). The original "Scavenger HuntÂ©" was designed for the larger screen. If you have the wrong version, simply write or call ImagiNation Enterprises and we will gladly send you the other version. This program is SHAREWARE, so if you decide to keep it, please send the amount of $5.00 to ImagiNation Enterprises at the following address: ImagiNation Enterprises 463 N 800 E American Fork, Utah 84003-1919 (801) 763-0682 This program is actually a HyperCardâ¢ stack written in HyperCardâ¢ 2.2, and requires HyperCardâ¢ 2.0 or newer to run it. If you have any questions or suggestions, please send them to ImagiNation Enterprises! All rights reserved. Â©1996 by ImagiNation Enterprises

The software includes the RV number, key, and the title and instrumentation of the work, as well as search capabilities. This is a demo version which only contains a partial catalogue and no online help (for space considerations). If you'd like the complete version, please fill out the registration card contained in the main stack and return the printed form along with $15 to us. You will receive the complete version, with sounds included, within 2-3 weeks. Copyright (c)1993, Software Perspectives. All Rights Reserved.

title: Spanish Flash Card
description: This is a very interactive hypercard stack which teaches very young kids math with positive numbers in Spanish. With the random generator there are millions of questions and possible answers. A question is presented visually and spoken in Spanish and three possible choices are give. You have 2 tries. If you get it wrong twice you are shown how the problem is done interactively with sound and pictures. You then correct your mistake and continue on. I will download the French and German versions of this program sometime in the near future. This is shareware so please feel free to try it out.
