title: ReadCat XFCN.SIT
description: ----------------- The ReadCat XFCN will give you handy access to much of the information in the floppy disk catalogs to use as you see fit in your stacks. You may display the catalog brazenly as I do in my stack, Auto Floppy Log 3.5, or you may elect to protect your users from this unpleasantness by using the information as a skeleton for fancy graphic representations. The ReadCat XFCN may be used in several modes. The simplest is a HyperTalk statement such as 'put ReadCat() into catbox.' This will cause HyperCard to prompt the user to insert a floppy disk which will then be read. The catalog will end up in the variable 'catbox' or any other container, in the following format: Line 1: The Volume Name of The Floppy Disk Lines 2 to (The Number of Files and Folders on The Disk): There will be 7 comma delimited items in each line as follows: Item 1: Will be either 'File' or 'Fldr' depending on which it is. Item 2: Will contain the short Date Last Modified of the file. Item 3: The Size of the File in KiloBytes. Item 4: The File Name. Item 5: The Directory ID number (see below). Item 6: The Parent ID number (Yup, parents too, see below). Item 7: The File Type, like 'APPL', 'TEXT', 'STAK'
modernAnalog: external drive or folder manifest importer for file inventory/logging
coreUserJob: Read a storage volume and turn its file/folder catalog into structured records for display, logging, and downstream UI use.
whyRelevant: This is a durable intake-and-normalization pattern: prompt for a storage source, read directory metadata, emit a structured manifest, and let another layer render it plainly or as a richer interface.
likelyStructure: User selects or inserts storage source; system scans volume; outputs header plus repeated file/folder rows with metadata and parent-child IDs; downstream stack stores, displays, or skins the manifest.

title: +GNC HyperTour v. 2.3
description: Staff Pick -- a true labor of love. Beautifully done, and quite interesting even if you have no passion for model trains. GNC HyperTour 2.1 is a Macintosh HyperCard 2.x tour of my model railroad, the Great Northern Cascade Railway. The GNC is an N-scale layout measuring 11.5 x 14.5 feet overall, and has a 75.5 foot mainline, approximately 2-1/4 scale miles. It represents a freelanced version of the Great Northern's Wenatchee-Oroville branch in North Central Washington state. This file is a StuffIt archive and AOL software should unstuff it for you automatically. It unloads to a 213K HyperCard stack, which requires a Macintosh running HyperCard 2.x. For those not able to view this stack, please keep your eyes open for an updated GIF file, GNC Today.GIF, which presents a simplified trackplan of the GNC. This HyperTour is the _third_ version of the tour I have posted here. This stack has been checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with Virex 5.6

likelyStructure: landing overview, sequential tour stops, annotated images or trackplan, contextual notes, optional simplified map

title: Space-Aligned Columns
description: This utility converts tab-delimited text from a database or word processor into space-separated columns, with each tab replaced by the correct number of (monospace-font) spaces to align the columns. The original purpose of this utility was to be able to use the "PRE" HTML tag to properly display columns of tab-delimited data on a web browser, but it is useful for any situation where tabs must be replaced. This is freeware. First, paste the tabbed text into this field (the tabs will show as spaces, but they're still there). Then click the "No. of Columns" button and enter the number, then click the "Widths" button which will ask you for column widths (number of characters), which should be entered separated by commas. Then click "Justify" if you want anything other than left justified columns. Each column can have its own justification; left, center, or right. Then click "Add space" if you need to insert extra space between any columns (such as a left-justified column next to a right-justified column). Finally "Convert" will do the conversion, take you to the next page, and select it all in preparation for copying and pasting. If the total number of characters in each row is wider than the window, it won't look good yet - you have to paste it into a wide-enough window to see the true results. As is often the case, creating this solution took longer than putting in spaces by hand in the original situation. But, if I ever have to do it again, now I have a tool. And so do you.
modernAnalog: fixed-width text formatter for exporting tabular data into plain-text reports or console-ready tables
coreUserJob: turn tab-delimited data into readable fixed-width text output with controlled alignment and spacing
whyRelevant: The durable job is still common: preparing structured plain-text tables for terminals, logs, emails, documentation, and other monospace outputs where tab rendering is inconsistent or unavailable.
likelyStructure: paste/import delimited text, define column count and widths, set per-column justification and spacing, generate formatted plain-text output for copy/export

title: DefaultCurs 1.01
description: ----------------- DefaultCurs is a utility for changing the standard HyperCard cursor in any stack from the Hand to any other predefined cursor. Unlike other utilities, such as the 'changecursor' XCMDs that change the cursor only until reset by HyperCard, DefaultCurs changes the standard cursor permanently for any stack in which it is installed. The effects of DefaultCurs can be reversed by removing defaultCurs' resources from the stack. DefaultCurs is useful, for example, if you want to use the arrow as the standard/default cursor in a stack, thus making it conform more closely to traditional Mac interface guidelines. The demo stack uses the arrow as the default cursor, but includes instructions for making any other cursor the default. Note: this does not work in HC 2.0, and probably won't in the future either (the docs warn about this).

title: 50 Filters for WindowScript(D...
description: 'Fifty Filters To Go' contains 50 keyFilter resources for use with WindowScript window fields in the HyperCard environment. WindowScript is available from Heizer Software. A WindowScript key filter is a compiled resource. Key filters can be assigned to a WindowScript window field. Key filters restrict certain characters from being entered into a field or that the data being entered matches a certain format or pattern, such as a telephone number or date. In otherwords, key filters ensure that only certain types of data can be entered into a field. Unlike other key filters, the key filters in 50 Filters To Go don't filter anything. BUT they do call HyperTalk handlers designed by you to filter the data. You can create your own custom keyFilters without having to learn or use a compiler like C or Pascal. You can also test and debug your key filters immediately in HyperCard's run-time, interactive environment. The filters in this stack work exactly like the real thing. Try writing your own Key Filters for WindowScript fields in HyperTalk! --------------------------- Checked with Disinfectant 3.6

PeekByte, PeekWord, and PeekLong that take an address and return the value stored at that address. These functions all contain numerous options that enhance their utility. TrapName, TrapWord for converting the name of a trap to its corresponding trap word and vice versa. DecToHex and HexToDec for converting between decimal and hexadecimal values. And more. However, with each stack I looked into, the more frustrated I became because I had to do a bunch of searching to find out how things worked. Then came an article in MacUser. Eureka! Part of my dreams were answered. No, I did not meet the woman I would marry, but I found a script that would help me look at a stack's inner workings. Unfortunately, I had to print the results the script put out and then go into the analyzed stack to do my search - even that seemed confusing. But I wanted more! (Don't they always want more??) Thus was born the thought of this stack. It has saved me a lot of headaches (and created a few of its own!). It is not the easiest stack to learn, but once you have used it, it becomes necessary if you want to learn some of the tricks people are using in their stacks without lots of random and frustrating exploration. I recommend you read the HELP card first??? Click on any '?' icon for help. Don't be afraid to LEARN!!!! I think there are several bugs that are still in the scripts, so please don't swear at me if it doesn't work properly. I'm not perfect either! I'm working on them! Let me know if you find bugs!

title: Audio Sequencer!
description: Audio Sequencer is a HyperCard stack created as a poor-man's sequencer. With no interface with MIDI or anything else for that matter, it's accuracy is partially dependent on your reflexes. Designed to play short sounds, such as sound effects (gunshots, etc.), it functions very well as a low end sequencer. Okay, maybe it's not really a sequencer, but it does have the ability to play a sound (or a series of sounds) at the exact moment you want it to. Designed with the synching to video in mind, the standard time unit is one frame of video (1/30th of a second). Each card has a time ruler of two seconds, with sixty units on the ruler. A sound can be played on any one of those units. Concievably, you could play a different sound on every frame (who knows what it would sound like though!) You can have as many cards as you want, each representing two seconds. Or you can have one card represent several seconds. You can have as many sounds play over as long of time as you want (memory permitting). Actually, the time limit is 59:59:29, being 59 minutes, 59 seconds, and 29 frames. This hasn't been tested to work though (who has the time?) I've even surprised myself on this stack, it works really well and doesn't cost much (an e-mail registers you)! So why not give it a try all you home video and audio buffs, this may be just what you need to give some life to your masterpieces!

coreUserJob: place and trigger short audio clips at precise frame-based times against a video timeline
whyRelevant: This maps cleanly to a durable media-production job: cueing and sequencing sound effects on a timeline with frame precision. The card-based two-second segments also suggest a reusable modular structure for building longer cue tracks from repeatable timeline units.

title: doTitles & curlyQuotes XFCNs
description: doTitles & curlyQuotes XFCNs Sunday, June 21, 1998 -------------------------------------------------------------------- doTitles XFCN (v. 1.0) and curlyQuotes XFCN (v. 1.0) -------------------------------------------------------------------- Size, expanded: 65K. Public domain XFCNs. -------------------------------------------------------------------- doTitles() XFCN id #31611 v. 1.0 This external function capitalizes words in titles. Unlike other functions and scripts I've seen, it doesn't just capitalize the first letter of each word indiscriminately. Instead, it capitalizes the first and last words of the title and all nouns, pronouns, adjectives, verbs, adverbs, and subordinate conjunctions; it does not capitalize articles, coordinate conjunctions, or prepositions, regardless of length, unless they are the first or last words of the title. If the title contains a subtitle preceded by a colon, it capitalizes the first word of the subtitle too. If the title contains only capital letters or has otherwise been capitalized improperly, it recapitalizes according to the criteria above. If there are straight up-and-down quotation marks around the title (') or odd substitutions for quotation marks (`` or ''), it converts them to curved, left- and right-handed marks. It also converts single quotation marks and apostrophes. It's smart enough to recognize some contractions ('til, 'twas, and so forth), but not all possible contractions. It capitalizes abbreviations if they contain periods (U.S.A. or Ph.D.), but not if they don't (ACLU or SJ). (That's because it doesn't know whether ACLU is a word or an abbreviation.) It converts double hyphens (--) into real em dashes, and hyphens between dates and numbers in series (1901-76) into real en dashes. Finally, it converts double or multiple spaces into single spaces. curlyQuotes() XFCN id #19501 v. 1.0 This function also converts quotation marks and apostrophes to the curved variety, but it does nothing else. ------------------------------------------------------------------- Syntax: put doTitles(container) into container put curlyQuotes(container) into container -where container is any HyperCard container, such as a field or variable. ------------------------------------------------------------------- doTitles and curlyQuotes come installed in a demonstration stack. To install these external functions in another stack, you must use a resource-copying utility, such as ResCopy, ResEdit, or ResMover. -------------------------------------------------------------------

title: House of Multiplication Horro...
description: The House of Multiplication Horror AlphaDog SoftWare by Glen L. Bledsoe Just in time for Halloween! This stack is a big improvement over flashcards -- including my previous 'Flashy Flashcards.' It can used by an individual, but was designed especially for use by a classroom. If the answer is correct a random visual effect will take place and a creepy sound will be heard. If the answer is wrong, the correct answer will be shown in the message box. If a student takes too long to answer the fact, even if his or her answer is correct, they will not get credit for having mastered the fact, but will receive a reward for having gotten the right answer. This stack discards the facts that students already know and proceeds to drill them in groups of four only on the facts that they have shown that they do not know or do not know quickly. After the student demonstrates that he or she knows a given fact well (by getting it correct 4 times while being shuffled in with 3 other facts), that fact is discarded and is replaced by another fact that the student had trouble with previously until all 91 multiplication facts are mastered. Research indicates that this is a very effective way to use flashcards. This stack will not forget where a student left off in his or her practice from day to day. They can come back at any time and pick up where they left off. This stack works best having the PostCrypt Font in your System Folder! It is available elsewhere on AOL. This is Freeware! Share is with anyone who can use it.

coreUserJob: help students master multiplication facts through adaptive repeated practice that tracks speed, accuracy, and progress over time

title: Disk-it.sit
description: This stack is very useful and easy to use. Basically what this stack does is keep a catalog of your floppy disks. All you do is create a new card by pressing the 'New Card' button. The underneath 'Set Disk Size' is there merely to calculate the free size left on your disk. The default setting is for 800k disks. If you want to catalog a 400k disk just press the button next to the field. If you don't know what size disk you are using, leave it set to 800 - no big deal. Next press the 'Catalog Disk' button. This brings up a dialog box requesting you insert a floppy disk - it does the rest! When the catolog is finished your disk will pop out. Just follow the same procedure to catolog your other disks. If your disks change information a lot, go back to the card which you want to re-catalog and start the same procedure over again. The fields will be written-over by the new information, there is no need to erase fields (although you are perfectly welcome to change information by editing fields). The Size of Disk field near the bottom of the card shows your total memory used up on that disk. The Free Size field below it shows the amount of memory left. Also, on the main card, an easy way to go to a particular card or view what cards (disks) you have, just press the 'Table of Contents' button. A dialog will pop up requesting you choose a card to navigate to. If you just want to view the cards (disks) and not go to one then simply choose 'Cancel' and you will be returned to the main card. To print a report with all the contents of your floppies just press the 'Print Report' button. The print dialog box will pop-up and all you have to do is press 'OK'. Normally don't worry about changing anything unless you're not happy with the report.

title: ShowDialog XCMD 1.6.7r
description: ShowDialog XCMD 1.6.7r Sunday, June 21, 1998 A revised version of the late Jay Hodgdon's ShowDialog 1.6.7 stack, with updated instructions and registration information. The ResEdit instructions have been rewritten for 2.1.1. THE EXTERNALS THEMSELVES HAVE NOT BEEN CHANGED. Includes several bonus externals. From the stack: 'ShowDialog XCMD is an external command that will allow you to display ANY type of dialog or alert that can contain ANY number of buttons, ANY amount of text, and ANY number of icons or PICT resources. You decide whether the dialog or alert is to have a default (outlined) button. You can have as many fields of static text as you wish and can modify any two of these on the fly (in the XCMD parameter list). You can have any number of editable text fields that the user can type into. Any of these fields can contain a default text string (that you specify in the parameter list). Your dialog can have as many check boxes or radio buttons as you want or can have a popup menu or a scrolling list from which the user can select items. You can also create specialized dialogs for the display of interactive help screens, color icon bars, or Macintosh 'splash screens.' You use ResEdit to create the necessary DLOG/DITL resources that allow ShowDialog to function. ShowDialog XCMD takes seven forms: Type 1, Type 2, Type 3, Type 4, Type 5, Type 6, and Type 7. Type 1 allows for 'regular' buttons, icons, PICTs, and static text only. You can modify the text as you wish in the parameter list. Type 2 can have these and ALSO any number of editable text fields that the user can type into and an unlimited number of check boxes. Any of these fields can also contain default text (provided in the parameters). Type 3 shares the same attributes as Type 2 with the exception that you can have as many radio buttons as you want instead of the check boxes. Type 4 is also similar to Type 2 with the exception that this type contains a popup menu. Types 5 and 6 are used to display 'help' screens consisting of multiple PICTs. Type 7 displays a scrolling field from which the user can select lines in a fashion similar to the Font/DA Mover. You specify the font, pt. size, and default line of the list in the parameter list. This type can also respond to any number of 'regular' buttons. (Editor's note, Type 7 ShowDialogs do not work properly under System 7.)' Edited and originally uploaded by Perry Barton (PBARTON).

title: Modeless Dialogs
description: ----------------- ABOUT: Modeless Dialogs is a collection of four intelligent XFCN's (external functions) that allow HyperCard developers to create modeless and modal dialogs. The XFCN's included in this stack will allow you to create/display as many modeless and/or modal dialogs as you wish, monitor activities relating to the dialogs, and dispose the dialogs when you are through. Using these XFCN's is like using any HyperTalk command. It's that intuitive! WHAT IS A MODELESS DIALOG? Simply put, a modeless dialog is a dialog that can remain on the screen without forcing the user to respond to it before continuing to work. While a modeless dialog is up, a user can still work with HyperCard as if the dialog was not there; typing, navigating etc...Similar to the tool and pattern palettes in HyperCard. EXAMPLES: Modeless dialogs can be used to create button panels that are relocatable by the user (they can even be placed outside the HyperCard window so large screen monitors can be fully utilized). You can use color pictures (PICT resources) in your dialogs and respond to mouse clicks on it too. The uses are really limited only to your imagination. USE: You are free to use these XFCN's for any personal or commercial stackware. All we ask is that you please make a tax-deductible donation to one of the charities listed in the stack. Another intelligent product from Intelligent Solutions!

title: EZ Bike Gear Calculator
description: An attractive, fun, and very easy to use and understand program, THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR will automatically figure out the 'gear/inches' for any bicycle's front chainwheel/rear cog combinations. It also contains a complete easy to read description and history of just what 'gear/inches' are (and why you should care), for those who aren't familiar with the term. Determining and installing the proper selection of gears on your bike will enable you to cope with the enormous variations you may encounter in your riding, not only in road and wind conditions, but also in your body's ability to produce power. THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR is necessary because, unfortunately, the selection of gears on most 'factory-built' bikes is chosen mostly in terms of the manufacturer's convenience, and also, of course, must necessarily reflect an 'average' of all the thousands of riders who buy that brand and model of bike. However, in real life, a dedicated rider with years of experience is going to require a different set-up from a casual novice, and, of course, a rider in a flat area like Florida, for instance, will require a totally different range of gears from a rider in a mountainous area like, say, Colorado. I'm sure that by now you've 'personalized' your bike, choosing gadgets and accessories that reflect your own personal taste. Well, taking it one step further, and insuring that the selection of gears installed on your bike is the selection that's exactly right for you and your riding style, is where THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR comes in. Suitable for both road and mountain bikes, THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR can process data for up to 3 front chainwheels and 8 rear cogs. Using THE GEAR/INCH CALCULATOR to figure out the 'gear/inches' of your present set-up will help you easily determine whether any of your present chainwheel/cog combinations are duplicates, whether your high gear is high enough, your low gear low enough, or if any of the combinations fall outside the range of gearing that's suitable for your riding style and conditions, as well as the best shift pattern to use to 'step' through your range of gears. title: Hackintosh 1.7
description: Hackintosh is a cool program I wrote whilst completely toasted on caffeine. (Come to think of it, most of my software is like that. You don't think 10-12 cups of coffee a day is too much, do you?) I designed Hackintosh for a friend of mine. He had an old laptop (5300c@117MHz) and the Finder really crawled. So, he asked me if I would write a command-line for him so he could work with his files more quickly. I picked Linux as my model, and HyperCard as my platform. For the file access, I chose to use an adaptation of some HyperTalk/AppleScript modules I'd written (which eventually became Hackintosh Core Services). Although the first version used the shell commands directly via 'start using,' subsequent versions have used a Virtual Memory Module, to load an almost unlimited number of shell commands. What started out as a quick hack for navigating and manipulating files quickly turned into something far more powerful. Because of the inherent extendability of the HyperTalk scripting language, plus the OSA and XCMD/XFCN interface, Hackintosh can be programmed to do just about anything, completely via shell commands. You can write shell commands for complex file management, resource control, even control over the internet. In fact, if you can find/write/hack a way to do something in HyperCard, you can probably make Hackintosh do it.
modernAnalog: scriptable terminal workspace for file operations and shell-task automation
coreUserJob: navigate, manipulate, and automate files and system tasks quickly through typed commands instead of slow graphical navigation

title: Pathbased Animations
description: Getting tired of having to write huge scripts to tell hypercard how to animate your buttons? Wish you could make fluid, flowing animations with your icons? Have you ever wanted the computer to write words on the screen, or paint a picture with a single line of code? If any of those are true, or if you're just looking for a fantastic utility, 'Dragger' is for you! Dragger is a simple, but effective utility for creating fields and fields of paths. Simply drag the mouse where you want the path. Dragger will automatically show you where your points are on your path. When you're done, release the mouse, and dragger will do all the calculations and give you two fields full of points and commands. If some of this isn't making sense, don't worry! Included in this stack are several demonstration buttons that will show you exactly how to use these paths to animate your button!. That's right, just point, click, drag, and you're done! Then you can go back into your stack, and have your buttons move quickly or slowly across the path you've dragged for them! Because Dragger has a constant rate of recording, you can control the speed of your animations for your buttons, by simply slowing down or speeding up your mouse! Download this fantastic utility now! ===================== Checked for Mac and HyperCard viruses with Virex 5.6

title: Creepin' Critter Math 4.0
description: Creepin' Critter Math is an arcade-style learning game which provides children with math practice in a way that is both challenging and fun. They swat bugs (the "Creepin' Critters") or pop bubbles that correspond to the correct answer to a math problem. Points are earned, in true arcade game style, for correct answers. The amount of time available to answer a problem can be adjusted to suit the math skill level of the child. The arcade-style scoring provides an incentive for decreasing the amount of time, since lower time settings increase the possible scores. 1) COUNTING objects up to 10; 2) BEGINNER Math addition and subtraction problems involving numbers no higher than 10; 3) BASIC math facts for addition, subtraction, multiplication and division; and 4) ADVANCED two step problems for older children which involve carrying, borrowing and remainders.

title: StringToNum XFCN 1.2.sit
description: ----------------- StringToNum will strip a string, such as one containing a currency symbol, of all text and convert it to math-suitable numbers. Parentheses around the number will be correctly interpreted as a negative number. International decimal characters are supported. The XFCN is intelligent enough to ignore any parentheses or minus signs and periods within a text portion of the string: only those directly around a number or international currency symbol will be accepted. =George=

title: ChangeObjectLayer.SIT
description: While developing a stack containing numerous fields and buttons, I found myself constantly using the Send Farther and Bring Closer commands in the Objects menu in order to move objects (buttons and fields) through different card and background layers. In some cases I wanted to obscure other objects, and in other cases I wanted to prevent objects from being obscured. Well, when you have 20 or more each of background and card buttons and fields, this menu selection (or its command key equivalent) business to change object layers can become quite tedious, boring, and unfriendly. Therefore, I developed 'ChangeObjectLayer' to automate and speed-up the process of moving objects through different layers. 'ChangeObjectLayer' works with both card and background buttons and fields. Try it out! On this card are six each of card and background buttons and fields. Click on any one of these objects to change its order in the object layers.

title: Library Stack 1.1.SIT
description: ----------------- The Library Stack is a disk catalogger. It will read the Volume Name, File Name, File Size, and File Type from your floppy disks and put each file on a card. It also attempts to group files with similar names on the same card. It also has a place for you to write keywords and a description. It has sorting and searching capabilities. Shareware. This is an updated version of Library Stack 1.0. It fixes a bug in the find buttons. If all of the associated files have the same name, they should end up on the same card. If they have different names, then they will end up on different cards and you will have to group them manually. Version 1.0 put each file on a separate card and they all had to be grouped manually. It also ignores the folder names, desktop files, and the information type files on Educorp disks which previously ended up on separate cards. In the future I hope to find a better way to group the files so that whatever files are grouped in a folder will end up on the same card.

description: This program simulates reservoir operations on the Columbia River system. Several species of salmon have been declared threatened or endangered in the river system. A fun simulation game that lets you operate a hydro power reservoir and try to save the Northwest salmon! See the impacts on recreation, flooding, power, and fish of your own strategy for operating the reservoir. This is a fun learning tool and will give useful insight into how reservoirs operate, and also the kinds of social tradeoffs that have to be made in operating the reservoirs. The issues raised in this program are real life issues being addressed by reservoir operators in the Pacific Northwest. This program is an upgrade of an earlier version. The primary change is you can now keep track of your reservoir operation through the year, and print out a graph of the operation and the relative benefits. title: DefaultCurs.SIT
description: DefaultCurs Stack 1.01 by Dean Wette, XNet 27 February 1989 Have you ever wished you could change the default cursor in HyperCard from the hand to something more normal, like an arrow? Me too. In fact I don't like the hand...it looks stupid, especially in a stack where you've spent a lot of time constructing an elegant user interface. If you don't agree then you might as well go to the 'File' menu above and select 'Delete Stack'. Otherwise, if you want to get rid of the 'hand' cursor in your stack permanently without the necessity of an inefficient or impractical idle handler, then read on... The hand cursor in HyperCard actually lives in 2 resources. The first, and most difficult to alter, is the (until now) permanent default cursor for HyperCard. This cursor and it's mask are in the '_12' font in HyperCard. The other 'hand' cursor looks exactly the same; it's the one used when you give the command, 'set cursor to hand,' from a script. It's a real CURS resource. To permanently change the default cursor to something other than the hand, you will have to do three things: edit the CURS resource and edit the '_12' font, and, finally, you have to include the following command in your openStack handler to activate the new default cursor: 'choose browse tool'. This will insure that the real hand is purged for the new default cursor. This editing of the default cursor was done with HyperCard 1.2.2. I can't guarantee it will work with future versions of HyperCard. All that needs to happen is for Bill to change the font ID or the way the cursor works and everything's gone to hell-in-a-handbasket. So keep that in mind.

whyRelevant: This maps to a durable UI customization job: overriding default interaction affordances to fit a product's design system and workflow state. The pattern is still relevant, but it is narrow and implementation-specific rather than a broad end-user module.

title: PaletteXFCN.SIT
description: The Palette XFCN by Oscar F. Hills 1988 Version 1.03b1 This XFCN was actually written after a conversation at the Boston Mac Expo in which a gentleman who shall remain nameless almost convinced me that it was impossible to write a reasonable HyperCard external which would allow the user to install an additional Pull-Down, Tear-Off menu like the 'Tools' menu, but one in which the user could customize the selections and have them do anything he/she wanted. I then decided to undertake the challenge, and have been working on it ever since. The result is here before you, something in between a kludgey workaround and a nice addition to HyperTalk. As in the 'Tools' menu, the little tool icons are actually font characters which you can change into anything you wish using ResEdit or a Font Editor. Then you can write HyperTalk scripts to define what each of the 18 selections will do. The menu can be pulled down, torn off, hidden, and shown exactly like the 'Tools' (or 'Pattern') menu. There are additional features explained later, which the 'Tools' menu does not have.

title: HyperTools Stack 1.0
description: eleteMenu all allow you to modify a menu that was created with makeMenu() or in a script of a stack. ThisStack() returns the name of the current stack. SF() displays a prompt and a listing of files to open of the defined type. TriList() prints out a list of pythagorean triangles. Qs() returns the word(s) you put in its parameter in quotes. FuncErr() returns a function error (if you made a mistake defining a function). IsPythagorean() and pTriples() Tell you whether a triangle is a right triangle (Pythagorean) and find the missing side. ProcessErr() displays the error , beeps how many times you ask it to, displays the desired buttons, and exits to HyperCard(optional). And finally, aboutStack displays the desired 'answer' and string for the stack you want it too. Hope you find these utilities useful and remember to support shareware. *************************************************** Checked with CP Anti-Virus 3.0 for viruses

title: MouseUp 1.5
description: MouseUp is a Hypercard stack to manage data for a transgenic mouse colony. Its particular purpose is to generate an index file of the individual mice in the colony. Each mouse has an index card listing the mouse's ID, sex, birthdate, genotype, parents, and offspring. Also there is a field for entering any text as notes. Other features include ability to search the database for any entry in any field, and automatic updating of the offspring field of parent mice when new information is entered. But the coolest thing is that the mouse database is a hypertext family tree. In other words, there is a link from any mouse's card to the cards of its parents and its offspring. Just point and click the mother listed on a mouse's card (for example) and jump immediately to the card of the mother mouse. Then click the mouse you just came from, listed under offspring, and go back.

CountNulls() is an xfcn that tells you how many nulls are in a designated file. NoNulls is an xcmd that allows you to either replace all nulls in a file with a replacement string of your choice, or replace all occurrences of a string with nulls. title: Pi In The Sky
description: Pi In The Sky October 23, 1994 This freeware stack is an update of the earlier stack. It includes a Pi menu and Help screens that are keyed to the active card. The stack is not protected but the user level has been set to painting to prevent accidental damage. You can change the user level by means of the Protect Stack menu item. If you don't see the menu item in the HyperCard File menu then hold the command key while you mouse the File menu open. The stack script also includes several handlers that are used to dynamically modify the script under certain conditions. The stack can be used to begin to explore the development of the concept of pi. The stack gets its title from the book Mathematics For The Million by Lancelot Hogben. The title was created with Aldus Type Twister. Other sources are listed in the stack. The value of pi is approximately 3.14159. Archimedes calculated the value as more than 3 1/7th and less than 3 10/71s. Computers have been used to calculate the value of pi to many decimal places. HyperCard internally uses 3.14159265358979323846 as its value for pi. I would appreciate any and all comments and suggestions. You can contact me by mail at: Arthur V. Dieli, Harbor Road, Henderson Harbor, NY, 13651-0009 or by email at: ArthurD12@AOL.com or DieliAV@eWorld.com.

title: LinksBase 1.1
description: A HyperCard database for web directories. LinksBase allows you to maintain a database of links and HTML code templates, and build specialized pages out of them automatically. It is really only meant for building pages that are, essentially, lists of links, like Yahoo! for example (although it would be utterly innapropriate for Yahoo! itself): it doesn't have much to offer someone posting poetry to the Web. I designed LinksBase mostly for my own benefit, and the cheesy interface should be a dead giveaway that I wasn't thinking too hard about commercial potential. This stack will be of most use to someone who is reasonably comfortable with both HTML and HyperTalk: most people will not want to use the templates I have provided, and in fact, to get the most out of it, you may want to add a few fields, which will necessitate adding some new code. The stack comprises four main parts: the Detail cards, which is where you keep information on individual links, the Categories cards, which is where you set up a page-level and subcategory-level hierarchy, the HTML Code cards, where you insert chunks of HTML code to be used as templates, and the Build card, where you make it all happen.

title: AddTimes XFCN
description: ----------------- AddTimes XFCN will add together a list of time entries. You can use return-delimited or item (comma) delimited lists. The XFCN will correctly calculate negative time entries, so you can provide billing credits if necessary. You can also have the resource cancel the calculation and flag you if any colons are missing in the list. You don't need to include colons normally--the XFCN will accept integers. An added benefit is that the resource will strip text from the line, so you don't have to worry if there is a descriptive statement in front of each entry on the same line. However, if that text includes dashes or colons you may have problems, for obvious reasons. Use is free in non-commercial stacks, provided credit is given in the stack. Copyright 1990 by George Pytlik. All rights reserved.

title: Rites of Passage v2.2
description: READ ME! RITES OF PASSAGE A SCIENCE FICTION ROLEPLAYING MODULE ON HYPERCARD Rites of Passage is a series of HyperCard 2 stacks that comprise a systemless science fiction roleplaying module. You have just downloaded the Menu and Introduction/Character stack. The aim of the Rites Project is to release the entire module (including printable character sheets and GM notes) on HyperCard for free distribution and play. THE NATURE OF THE STACKS The two introductory stacks are large, each over 500K when decompressed. They together comprise 85 cards giving a fifteen minute sound, text and graphic introduction to the module followed by selections from the Characters� stack. The introductory stack does NOT contain the full module: it will be released after convention play in early 1993. Rites of Passage is written and scripted by John Hughes of The Wyrm�s Footprint. It is an experiment in electronic module design and distribution. A convention version of the module will run at CanCon (Canberra, Australia) in January 1993. The completed stacks will be available through the Net shortly thereafter. The stack is postcardware, and may be freely distributed, as long as it is not altered in any way. If you have any suggestions for improvement � scientific, programming, dramatic or jest plain old english expression then PLEASE let me know. The introductory stack contains full information on the module and on the further stacks. E-mail me through InterNet at john.hughes@anu.edu.au Rites of Passage is a systemless and thematic module � a module designed to explore a number of themes or moral quandaries using mood, atmosphere and depth of characterisation. Set in the 24th Century, it is designed to explore the cultural and psychological impact of computer implants and virtual reality. While focussing on characterisation and drama, the module also features a murder mystery, a black hole, possible alien contact... oh, just download and explore the stack. HOW TO USE THE STACK Rites of Passage requires HyperCard 2 and System 6.07 or higher. It will run on any Mac with the necessary memory. As it uses a large number of digitised sounds, a minimum of 4 Meg RAM is recommended. The Introduction (and subsequent stacks) use resources stored in the Menu stack, so they should not be separated. VERSION 2.1 Versions 1.x of these stacks used MacIntalk, a voice generating utility no longer supported by Apple. The MacIntalk scripts caused system crashes in certain Mac II machines, and they have been deleted in the current version. In addition, several cards have been added or clarified in the current version. The original large Introduction has been split into two stacks. Oh yeah, and the spelling... I'd be grateful for comments or reviews. REALLY. Please mail me at the address above. Free Information! Free Imagination! John Hughes

title: MacChef 2.0.3
description: I first wrote MacChef in 1995, after becoming frustrated with the limitations of a recipe program I had downloaded a few weeks earlier. Oh, the program worked all right, but it worked the way the writer wanted, not the way I wanted. If I rarely bake bread or pastry, why do I need a Bread & Pastry category of recipes? On the other hand, if I make a lot of pasta, why can't I add a Pasta category? MacChef allows me (and you) to do that kind of thing. I also wanted to make importing text files as easy as possible with a minimum of editing and reformatting. That gives MacChef a plain interface, but it saves time and effort when downloading recipes from the Web and the rec.food.recipes newsgroup, the source of many recipes in this collection. In the four years since I wrote the original MacChef, the number of recipes has grown to more than 500. I haven't tried all of them, but they all looked very promising when I downloaded them from rec.food.recipes and other sources. In each case, I've given credit to the original developer of the recipe if I knew who that person was. Many of the recipes include comments by the person who wrote the recipe. My own comments are attributed to "Bob." And you'll see several recipes I've developed myself. As a cardiac patient, I usually try to come up with recipes that are low in fat and sodium while still tasting great. (See my Low Fat Manicotti for example.)
modernAnalog: Custom recipe library with user-defined categories, text import, attribution, and personal notes
coreUserJob: Collect recipes from multiple text sources, organize them in personally meaningful categories, and annotate them with provenance and dietary notes

title: City to City
description: City to City is one of the best arguments for a laptop Mac to date. For people traveling the United Slates, Activision's new HyperWare offering gives a pretty thorough traveler's guide to 31 major cities, ranging from Honolulu to Boston and Seattle to Miami. In addition to making a wide range of resources available, City to City features a personal-profile card and customized itinerary planning. It also integrates with Activision's international travel stack, Business Sense. Making the best of the HyperCard interface, City to City begins with a map of the U.S. Clicking on a city brings up a card related to that metropolis. The 12 information categories include Business, Dining, Late- Night, Lodging, Market Data, Media, Shopping, Sports, Theaters, Tourism, Transport, and VisitorInfo. Buttons representing each of the categories are lined up along the bottom of each city card, so you can jump quickly from one category to another. You can also pick a different city from a pop-up list box. It's possible to preselect any category and go directly from the map to a specific topic for a certain city. Within each of the 12 categories is a wealth of information gleaned from the efforts of more than 30 researchers. The Lodging category, for instance, contains information about hotels, organized into three classes — First Class, Business Class, and Coach. The hotel's telephone number is listed (a toll-free number in many cases), and simply clicking on the Telephone button next to the number initiates a call to the selected hotel. You can also checkmark a hotel to indicate that you have a reservation and fill in the check-in and check-out dates, confirmation number (if any), and the daily rate you were quoted. With a click of the mouse, you can then transfer the necessary information to a current itinerary card. Should you discover a hidden gem of a hotel that isn't currently included in City to City, you can add it easily. In addition to the hotel information, each Lodging card contains a button that, when clicked, displays a map of the urban area with its major roads, airports, and the location of each hotel in the list. The hotels are represented by numbered buttons on the map and are also listed, by number, along the side of the map card. Clicking a button on the map takes you back to the main card containing that hotel’s information. The other categories contain equally useful features. Restaurants are listed by the kind of food they serve and are rated both by cost and by quality. Phone numbers are also listed, and you can copy restaurant data directly to itineraries. Emergency numbers, freight companies, convention centers, and much more can be found within the City to City stacks. I had an urge, on occasion, to see more information about specific items restaurants or hotels, for instance, or airline schedules — but that’s just the magic of HyperCard. You somehow begin to expect to see everything hyper-linked to more information. Obviously, City to City couldn't include a review of each restaurant and each hotel. With more than 10,000 entries and 7,500 phone numbers, the product is already impressive. Speaking as an inveterate telecommunicalor, I would have liked to see Telenet and Tymnet local-access numbers, at least, and even some good local BBS numbers. And the maps should display other special locations such as convention centers. The most versatile feature of City to City is the itinerary that you can easily develop as you browse through a city's cards. The itinerary is actually a stack. You tell the program the dates for your trip, and it creates a card for each day of your journey. The daily card has information about flights, lodgings, and car rentals as well as an appointment calendar. Other buttons open additional information about airlines, rental ears, and hotels (including appropriate toll-free numbers) as well as handle HyperCard functions related to itinerary cards and stacks. Finally, you cun add a special note card for any particular dale. My only complaint stems from the initial sentence of this review. City to City would be a great product to take along while traveling. Unfortunately, there is no affordable traveling Mac, so this wealth of useful material will have to stay home the next time I travel. I hope to get everything I need from it before I leave. DeMaria, Rusel. (September 1988). City to City. MacUser . (pgs. 57, 64).

title: HyperTrax Converter.SIT
description: ----------------- What is this thing? This is a utility stack which converts most HyperTrax,SoundWave, SoundCap and other digital sound files that are a.k.a. 'FSSD',into 'Snd' resources, which can be used for HyperCard stacks, Mac2 beeps or anywhere else you can call up the resource. It is input sensitive, but it is not output sensitive...so be careful! This is a resource editor, so handle it like a gun. I've seen grown men cry because they had thought it would be funny to put the theme to the 'Munsters' into their only copy of PageMaker. It is important that you keep in mind that digital sounds are RAM eaters. They do not get along well with other RAM dwellers such as talking mooses (meese?), screen savers, RAM cache's, trick clocks, etc...See what works with your system. Operation: The purpose of this program is to convert a sound file into a resource, and then install it on to a stack or application of your choice. Clicking within either the Source Sound icons or on the Source Path field, will prompt you to open a sound file. After selecting your sound, the Source Path will indicate the path to your selection. Target selection works the same way, only it will let you select ANYTHING you want to send the sound to.

title: RunTime Revolution
description: I can hear you now. "Oh, goodie. Yet another programming language. Just what we need. What do I need with another language? Isn't [insert your personal favorite language du jour here] good enough?" Leaving aside for the moment the question of why any serious software developer would confine him or herself to a single programming language, let's just say that if you want to develop Mac Classic and OS X applications that also happen to run virtually unchanged on Windows and *nix platforms, Revolution may just be the sweetest thing that's happened to you in a long, long time. If you're a seasoned professional programmer who writes only for the Macintosh, you may still find Revolution a useful tool. You can use it to prototype user interfaces quickly, turn those prototypes into demos for users to evaluate as you rapidly modify the UI in response to user feedback. If you're one of perhaps hundreds of thousands of people I call Inventive Users, you're going to love the ease of programming and application design in Revolution as well as the way it gets along with corporate databases, the Web, and rich media. (Inventive Users are people who don't program for a living but who are willing to take time to learn an accessible scripting language and use it to create applications they or their departments need or want.) What Is Revolution? Revolution, a product of Edinburgh, Scotland-based Runtime Revolution Ltd., grows out of HyperCard, a much-beloved and widely used Apple Computer product that the company abandoned a few years ago, much to the chagrin of tens of thousands of its customers, including some of the biggest companies in the world. HyperCard ran only on Macintoshes and was a strictly black-and-white application for most of its life. When color was added, near the end of the HyperCard life cycle, it was not well thought out and never worked very well. Scott Raney loved HyperCard but he needed something like it to run on Unix. So he set about to develop a HyperCard-like product for his platform of choice and wound up creating the MetaCard engine, about 10 years ago. Kevin Miller and his Scottish cohorts licensed the MetaCard engine, put a more robust and attractive programming UI on it and extended it in some other crucial ways, marketing the result as Revolution. Recently, Runtime Revolution acquired MetaCard and Raney now works for the engine's new owners. Revolution combines a powerful, graphical IDE (integrated development environment) with a highly accessible programming language called Transcript to create a platform for developing a broad range of software applications that will run virtually unchanged on Mac Classic, OS X, every flavor of Windows currently supported by Microsoft (and some that aren't) and many versions of *nix, including the widely popular Linux. Applications are in color, take full advantage of native UI widgets as much as possible, and offer performance comparable to Java and Basic applications. In fact, I like to call Revolution Java without the Java. Revolution delivers on the promise of true cross-platform development better than Java in my experience, and does so at a fraction of the programming time and effort. Shafer, Dan. (2004). Review: Runtime Revolution, Programming for Mere Mortals. MacTech. preserve.mactech.com/articles/mactech/Vol.20/ 20.05/RuntimeRevolution/index.html

title: BeeHive ADB I/O
description: With ADB I/O and HyperCard, SuperCard, AppleScript, Director or 4th Dimension, it is easy to build customized applications for your control and data acquisition needs. With the example code included in the package you can start controlling and logging data within minutes! Functional Features Plugs into the Apple Desktop Bus port on the Macintosh. 4 ADB I/O units can be connected to one Macintosh. No external power supply needed. 4 relay outputs. The relay outputs have a timer function that can be optionally set to, 0 to 25.5 seconds. 4 data acquisition channels. Each channel can be configured as an analog input (8 bits resolution), a digital input or a digital output. Maximum sample rate: 90 samples per second. 4 channels per sample. Max 12 samples per second recommended for smooth simultaneous mouse operation. - ( Bzzzzzz.com )
modernAnalog: desktop-configured industrial I/O and data-logging module for small control and monitoring workflows
coreUserJob: connect sensors and actuators to a computer so a custom app can read inputs, trigger outputs, and log measurements
whyRelevant: The durable job is still modern: low-channel hardware control, timed relay switching, signal acquisition, and event logging from a configurable software layer. The title and description imply a reusable module pattern of computer-connected I/O, channel configuration, polling, actuation, and logs.
likelyStructure: host-connected I/O adapter with configurable input/output channels, relay actions, timers, sampling, and logging hooks

title: Vocalise 1.0.2 XCMD
description: OPTIONAL: PlainTalk Text-toSpeech and matching voices SYSTEM 7: Compatible TYPE: Freeware, except for commercial use Replaces version 1.0.1 in MHC/HyperClones Vocalise is a powerful XCMD for use of Apple's Speech Manager. It allows you to change speed and pitch, pause the speaking at various points, stop speaking, find out what voices are available, and so on. * Version 1.0.2 introduces a number of changes to the stack, and has improved information on which files are needed for Vocalise and why. In addition, Vocalise now only requires System 6.0.7 or later. Happy 1.0 or 1.0.1 users may not want to upgrade, since there are no big changes. * A number of people have contacted the author asking where they can download the speech software from. This information is now in the stack: for those users with Internet and FTP access, there is information on the place you can download both the Speech Manager (with MacInTalk voices) and the PlainTalk software from.

title: MonthLong XFCN.sit
description: ----------------- MonthLong XFCN returns the number of days in a given month. You may already be using a HyperTalk or SuperTalk script for that; however this XFCN is faster and a little more versatile. For example, if you pass no parameters, it returns the number of days in the current month. If you pass the month only, it assumes the current year. Naturally, you can also supply the year. Best of all, you can pass the month as either a name (long or abbreviated) or an integer of any size. Non-commercial use of this XFCN is free. =George=
